title: Filing Requirements and Due Dates
description: Master the fundamental rules for who must file a federal income tax return and critical deadlines

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Filing requirements are the foundation of tax practice! As an Enrolled Agent, you MUST know who is required to file, when returns are due, and the consequences of late filing. These questions appear frequently on the SEE and are essential for serving clients.

sections.title: Who Must File a Federal Income Tax Return?
sections.content:
**General Rule:** You must file a federal income tax return if your gross income exceeds certain thresholds based on your filing status, age, and dependency status.

**2024 Filing Thresholds (Single Taxpayers):**
• Under 65: $14,600
• 65 or older: $16,550

**Married Filing Jointly:**
• Both under 65: $29,200
• One spouse 65+: $30,750
• Both 65+: $32,300

**Head of Household:**
• Under 65: $21,900
• 65 or older: $23,850

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Who ALWAYS Files
sections.content:
**"SENSE"** - You MUST file regardless of income if:

**S**elf-employment income ≥ $400
**E**mployer advance of EIC
**N**et earnings from church employee ≥ $108.28
**S**pecial taxes owed (AMT, household employment, etc.)
**E**xcess contributions to HSA or Archer MSA

**Also file if:** Received HSA/Archer MSA distribution, owe taxes on qualified plan distributions, or must recapture credits.

sections.title: Due Dates for Individual Returns
sections.content:
**Form 1040 Due Date:** April 15 following the close of the tax year

**If April 15 falls on weekend or holiday:** Due the next business day

**Examples for Tax Year 2024:**
• Regular due date: April 15, 2025
• Extended due date: October 15, 2025

**U.S. Citizens/Residents Abroad:**
• Automatic 2-month extension to June 15
• Must attach statement to return
• Interest still runs from April 15!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Extensions
sections.content:
**Form 4868 grants an AUTOMATIC 6-month extension to FILE, NOT to PAY!**

• Extension is to October 15
• Tax must still be paid by April 15
• Failure to pay = FTP penalty + interest
• No approval needed - just file Form 4868

**Estimated tax due:** Enter on Form 4868 line 4 and pay with filing.

sections.title: Combat Zone Extensions
sections.content:
**Special Rules for Military Personnel:**

Service in a designated combat zone extends deadlines:
• At least 180 days after leaving combat zone
• Plus any days remaining in the filing period when entering the zone

**Also applies to:**
• Hospitalization resulting from combat zone service
• Contingency operations

sections.title: Disaster Area Extensions
sections.content:
**Federally Declared Disaster Areas:**

The IRS may postpone deadlines for taxpayers affected by federally declared disasters. Check IRS announcements for:
• Specific affected areas
• Extended deadlines
• Which tax actions are postponed

**Common postponements:** Filing, payment, estimated taxes, IRA contributions

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Filing thresholds vary by filing status and age
Self-employment income ≥ $400 ALWAYS requires filing
Form 1040 is due April 15 (or next business day)
Form 4868 extends filing to October 15 (not payment!)
U.S. citizens abroad get automatic 2-month extension
Combat zone service significantly extends all deadlines
Always check for disaster relief announcements

knowledgeCheck.question: John, age 66 and single, received $15,000 in gross income during 2024. Is John required to file a federal income tax return?
options: Yes, because his gross income exceeds the filing threshold for single individuals under 65.
Yes, because his gross income exceeds the filing threshold for single individuals 65 or older.
No, because his gross income is less than the filing threshold for single individuals under 65.
No, because his gross income is less than the filing threshold for single individuals 65 or older.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The filing threshold for single individuals age 65 or older in 2024 is $16,550. Since John's gross income of $15,000 is less than this threshold, he is not required to file a federal income tax return.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider John's age and then compare his income to the correct filing threshold.

reveal.question: What is the regular due date for filing Form 1040 for the 2024 tax year?
reveal.answer: April 15, 2025. Remember to adjust if it falls on a weekend or holiday.

knowledgeCheck.question: April 15, 2025 falls on a Tuesday. What is the due date for filing Form 1040 for the 2024 tax year?
options: April 14, 2025
April 15, 2025
April 16, 2025
April 17, 2025
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since April 15, 2025 falls on a Tuesday, which is a business day, the due date for filing Form 1040 for the 2024 tax year is April 15, 2025.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider if April 15th is a weekend or holiday, if not, the 15th is the due date.

title: Filing Status Determination
description: Learn to correctly determine the most advantageous filing status for every taxpayer situation

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Filing status affects EVERYTHING: tax rates, standard deduction, eligibility for credits, phase-out thresholds. Choosing the wrong status can cost your client thousands of dollars or trigger an audit. This is one of the most heavily tested areas on the SEE!

sections.title: The Five Filing Statuses
rows: Married on 12/31 (or spouse died during year)
Unmarried + maintain home for qualifying person
Spouse died in prior 2 years + dependent child

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: HOH Requirements
sections.content:
**"HOME"** test for Head of Household:

**H**ome - You paid MORE than half the cost of keeping up the home
**O**ne dependent - A qualifying person lived with you more than half the year
**M**arital status - Unmarried (or considered unmarried) on 12/31
**E**xception - Your parent can live elsewhere if you pay 50%+ of their home costs

sections.title: Married Filing Jointly (MFJ)
sections.content:
**Requirements:**
• Legally married on December 31 of the tax year
• Both spouses agree to file jointly
• Can file jointly even if living apart

**Benefits:**
• Highest standard deduction ($29,200)
• Access to all credits (EIC, Education, etc.)
• Lower tax rates at all income levels

**Joint liability:** Both spouses are jointly and severally liable for the ENTIRE tax liability, even if only one spouse had income!

sections.title: Married Filing Separately (MFS)
sections.content:
**When MFS Makes Sense:**
• Spouse has significant unpaid taxes
• Spouse has questionable deductions
• Separation/divorce in progress
• Medical expenses (lower AGI = lower 7.5% floor)
• Student loan IBR plans

**MFS Restrictions ("Penalty" provisions):**
• NO earned income credit
• NO education credits
• NO child tax credit (if living with spouse)
• Social Security 85% taxable at lower thresholds
• Lower IRA contribution phase-outs

sections.title: Head of Household (HOH)
sections.content:
**Three Requirements - ALL must be met:**

**1. Unmarried** (or "considered unmarried") on December 31

**2. Paid more than half the cost of keeping up a home** for the year
• Rent, mortgage interest, property taxes, utilities, repairs, food
• NOT clothing, education, medical, vacations, life insurance

**3. Qualifying person lived with you more than half the year**
• Qualifying child (any age if disabled)
• Qualifying relative (MUST be related, not just any QR)
• EXCEPTION: Parent doesn't have to live with you!

sections.title: "Considered Unmarried" for HOH
sections.content:
**A MARRIED person can file HOH if ALL of these apply:**

1. Didn't live with spouse for the LAST 6 months of the year
2. Paid MORE than half the cost of the home
3. Home was the main home of qualifying child for MORE than half the year
4. Can claim the child as a dependent (or could but for custody agreement)

**Key point:** "Last 6 months" means July 1 - December 31, NOT any 6-month period!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: The Parent Exception
sections.content:
**For HOH status, a PARENT does NOT have to live with you!**

You can file HOH if:
• Your parent qualifies as your dependent
• You pay MORE than half the cost of their home
• Your parent can live in their own home, nursing home, etc.

**But wait!** For qualifying relatives (siblings, etc.), they MUST live with you the entire year to allow HOH status.

sections.title: Qualifying Surviving Spouse (QSS)
sections.content:
**Requirements (ALL must be met):**

1. Spouse died in one of the PRIOR two tax years
2. You were entitled to file MFJ the year spouse died
3. You didn't remarry before year-end
4. You have a dependent child (not adult)
5. You paid more than half the cost of keeping up the home
6. The home was the main home of your child the entire year

**Benefit:** Same tax rates and standard deduction as MFJ ($29,200)

sections.title: Filing Status Decision Tree
content: Step 1: Are you legally married on 12/31? → If no, consider Single or HOH
Step 2: If married, can you both agree to file jointly? → If yes, MFJ is usually best
Step 3: If married but filing separately may benefit, compare MFJ vs MFS
Step 4: If unmarried, do you meet HOH requirements? → If yes, file HOH
Step 5: Did your spouse die in the past 2 years with a dependent child? → QSS
items: **Married?**: If yes on 12/31, must file MJ or MFS (or HOH if abandoned).
**Spouse Deceased?**: Assume married for year of death.
**Dependents?**: If unmarried with dependents, check Head of Household.
**Spouse Died Last 2 Years?**: Check Qualifying Surviving Spouse (QSS).
**Default**: If unmarried and no dependents -> Single.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Filing status is determined on December 31 of the tax year
MFJ almost always produces the lowest tax for married couples
MFS severely restricts access to credits and favorable provisions
HOH requires unmarried + paid 50%+ of home + qualifying person
Parents don't have to live with you for HOH status
QSS available for 2 years after spouse's death (with dependent child)
'Considered unmarried' requires living apart the LAST 6 months of the year

knowledgeCheck.question: John and Mary were legally married on December 30th of the current tax year. They are currently living separately and disagree on how to file their taxes. John wants to file Married Filing Jointly to take advantage of the higher standard deduction, but Mary wants to file Married Filing Separately because she has significant medical expenses and believes this will lower her tax liability. Which of the following is TRUE?
options: They must file Married Filing Jointly because they were legally married on December 31st.
They must file Married Filing Separately because they disagree on how to file.
They can file Married Filing Jointly if both agree, even if living apart. If they don't agree, they must file Married Filing Separately.
They can choose whichever filing status results in the lowest overall tax liability.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: To file Married Filing Jointly, both spouses must agree. If they do not agree, they must file separately, regardless of whether they are living together or apart. The date of marriage is also important, but agreement is the key factor here.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the requirements for Married Filing Jointly.

reveal.question: Name at least two common tax benefits that are typically NOT available when filing as Married Filing Separately (MFS).
reveal.answer: Common restrictions when filing as MFS include ineligibility for the Earned Income Credit (EIC) and certain Education Credits (like the American Opportunity Tax Credit and Lifetime Learning Credit). Other restrictions may also apply depending on the specific tax situation.

rows.label: Standard Deduction
values: Highest Standard Deduction ($29,200)
Lower Standard Deduction (Half of MFJ, generally)

rows.label: Earned Income Credit (EIC)
values: Eligible (if other requirements met)
Generally Ineligible
rows.hint: EIC eligibility depends on income and dependents; MFS filers are usually restricted

rows.label: Joint Liability
values: Joint and Several Liability
Individual Liability
rows.hint: Joint and several means each spouse is responsible for the entire tax debt.

title: Dependency Requirements
description: Master the qualifying child and qualifying relative tests to correctly claim dependents

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Dependency claims are a major audit trigger! Correctly determining who qualifies as a dependent affects exemptions, filing status, CTC, EIC, and many other benefits. The SEE tests this extensively because it's where preparers make costly mistakes.

sections.title: Two Types of Dependents
sections.content:
**Every dependent must meet ONE of these two definitions:**

**1. Qualifying Child (QC)** - Generally children, stepchildren, foster children
• Uses the CARES test

**2. Qualifying Relative (QR)** - Other relatives (and sometimes non-relatives)
• Uses the SUPORT test

**Note:** A person can NEVER be both types - you test for QC first, then QR if QC fails.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Qualifying Child - "CARES"
sections.content:
**C**lose relative (child, stepchild, sibling, or descendant thereof)
**A**ge test (under 19, or under 24 if student, or any age if disabled)
**R**esidency (lived with you MORE than half the year)
**E**liminate joint return (child can't file MFJ unless only to claim refund)
**S**upport (child can't provide more than half their OWN support)

**Remember:** The "S" is about the CHILD providing their own support, not about YOU providing support!

sections.title: Qualifying Child Tests in Detail
rows: Child, stepchild, foster child, sibling, or their descendants
Under 19 at year-end, or under 24 if full-time student, or any age if permanently disabled
Same principal residence for MORE than half the year
Temporary absences count as living together
Cannot file MFJ with their spouse
Exception: filing only to claim refund
Child did NOT provide more than half their OWN support
Look at what CHILD paid, not parent

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Qualifying Relative - "SUPORT"
sections.content:
**S**upport - YOU provided MORE than half
**U**nder the gross income limit ($5,050 for 2024)
**P**recludes being a qualifying child of anyone
**O**nly certain relationships... OR
**R**esident of your household all year
**T**axpayer (joint return test)

**Key difference from QC:** For QR, YOU must provide >50% support, and there's a gross income limit!

sections.title: Qualifying Relative Tests in Detail
rows: Taxpayer provides MORE than half of dependent's support
Total support = food, shelter, clothing, medical, education, etc.
Cannot be a qualifying child of ANY taxpayer
Even if that taxpayer doesn't claim them
Related per IRC 152(d)(2) OR lives with taxpayer all year
Relatives: parents, siblings, in-laws, aunts/uncles, nieces/nephews

sections.title: The Support Test
sections.content:
**What Counts as Support:**
• Food (at home and away)
• Lodging (fair rental value if owned)
• Clothing
• Education
• Medical and dental care
• Recreation
• Transportation
• Similar necessities

**What Doesn't Count:**
• Life insurance premiums
• Funeral expenses
• Scholarships (for QC test - excluded from support calculation)
• Tax-exempt income (Social Security, welfare)

**IMPORTANT:** Scholarships are EXCLUDED from the support test for a qualifying child!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Multiple Support Agreement
sections.content:
**When NO ONE person provides >50% support:**

A Multiple Support Agreement (Form 2120) allows ONE person to claim the dependent if:

1. Combined support from the group exceeds 50%
2. Each individual would qualify EXCEPT for support test
3. The person claiming contributed MORE than 10%
4. Others who contributed >10% sign Form 2120 agreeing not to claim

**Common for elderly parents supported by multiple children.**

sections.title: Tie-Breaker Rules for Qualifying Child
sections.content:
**When a child qualifies for more than one taxpayer:**

**1. Parent vs. Non-Parent:**
• Parent WINS (child is treated as QC of parent)

**2. Both Parents (not filing jointly):**
• Parent with whom child lived LONGER wins
• If equal time, parent with HIGHER AGI wins

**3. Non-Parent vs. Non-Parent:**
• Person with HIGHER AGI wins

**4. Parent vs. Parent - Neither claims child:**
• Non-parent with highest AGI can claim

sections.title: Divorced and Separated Parents
sections.content:
**Custodial Parent (CP):** The parent with whom the child lived MORE nights during the year.

**Default Rule:** Custodial parent claims the child as dependent.

**Release of Claim (Form 8332):**
The custodial parent can release the dependency claim to the noncustodial parent:
• For the current year only, or
• For current and future years
• NCP can then claim child tax credit and dependency

**Custodial parent ALWAYS keeps:**
• HOH filing status (if otherwise qualified)
• Earned income credit
• Child and dependent care credit
• These CANNOT be transferred!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Qualifying Child uses CARES tests - age, relationship, residency, support (by child)
Qualifying Relative uses SUPORT tests - includes gross income limit (<$5,050)
For QC, the CHILD must not provide >50% of their OWN support
For QR, the TAXPAYER must provide >50% of the dependent's support
Scholarships are excluded from the QC support calculation
Tie-breakers: Parent wins, then longer residence, then higher AGI
Form 8332 releases dependency but NOT EIC, HOH, or dependent care credit

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding dependency exemptions?
options: A person meeting the requirements to be a qualifying child can also be a qualifying relative for a different taxpayer.
A person meeting the requirements to be a qualifying child CANNOT be a qualifying relative for ANY taxpayer.
A person can be both a qualifying child and a qualifying relative for the SAME taxpayer.
The CARES test is used to determine if someone is a qualifying relative.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is 'A person meeting the requirements to be a qualifying child CANNOT be a qualifying relative for ANY taxpayer.' This is a fundamental rule in dependency exemptions. If someone qualifies as a qualifying child, they cannot be claimed as a qualifying relative by anyone.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the hierarchy: Qualifying Child takes precedence.

reveal.question: List the elements of the CARES test used to determine if someone is a Qualifying Child.
reveal.answer:
C - Close Relative (Child, stepchild, foster child, sibling, half sibling, or descendant of any of these)
A - Age (Under 19 or under 24 if a student)
R - Residency (Same principal residence for more than half the year)
E - Eliminate Gross Income (Gross income less than the exemption amount)
S - Support (Did not provide more than half of their own support).

sections.title: Qualifying Child vs. Qualifying Relative: Support Test

rows.label: Support Provided By
values: The child must NOT provide more than half of their OWN support.
The taxpayer must provide MORE THAN half of the relative's support.

rows.label: Support Definition
values: Focus is on the child's OWN support.
Focus is on the taxpayer's contribution to the relative's support.

title: Taxpayer Identification Numbers
description: Understand the different types of tax identification numbers and when each applies

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Every tax return needs valid identification numbers. Using the wrong type of TIN or entering incorrect numbers causes delays, rejected e-files, and disallowed credits. As an EA, you'll help clients obtain the correct TINs and troubleshoot ID issues constantly.

sections.title: Social Security Number (SSN)
sections.content:
**Who Gets an SSN:**
• U.S. citizens
• Permanent residents (green card holders)
• Noncitizens authorized to work in the U.S.

**Key Points:**
• Required for EIC, CTC, ACTC, and most refundable credits
• Work-authorized SSN needed for many credits
• SSA assigns numbers; apply with Form SS-5
• SSN cards marked "Valid for work only with DHS authorization" limit credit eligibility

sections.title: Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN)
sections.content:
**Who Gets an ITIN:**
• Noncitizen spouse filing jointly
• Nonresident with U.S. tax obligation
• Dependents who aren't eligible for SSN
• Foreign investors with U.S. income

**Apply with Form W-7:**
• Submit with tax return (or exception applies)
• Must include passport or specified documents
• ITINs expire if not used for 3 consecutive years
• Middle two digits are 70-99 (identifying feature)

**Cannot be used for:**
• Work authorization
• EIC or refundable portion of CTC (for the worker)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: ITIN and Credits
sections.content:
**ITINs and Credit Eligibility:**

**CANNOT get EIC** with an ITIN (need valid SSN)

**CAN claim CTC** for a child with an SSN, even if parent has ITIN

**Dependent claimed** with ITIN → $500 Credit for Other Dependents (not CTC)

**Key:** The credit depends on the TIN of the person for whom it's claimed, not (usually) the claimer.

sections.title: Employer Identification Number (EIN)
sections.content:
**Who Needs an EIN:**
• Corporations and partnerships
• Estates and trusts
• Employers with employees
• Non-profits and other organizations
• Single-member LLCs with employees or excise tax

**How to Apply:**
• Form SS-4 (or online at IRS.gov)
• Immediate issuance online or by phone
• No cost to obtain
• One EIN per responsible party per day

**Format:** Two digits, hyphen, seven digits (XX-XXXXXXX)

sections.title: Adoption Taxpayer Identification Number (ATIN)
sections.content:
**When ATIN is Used:**
• Child placed for domestic adoption
• Adoption not yet final
• SSN cannot be obtained yet
• Need to file tax return claiming child

**Apply with Form W-7A:**
• Temporary number
• Expires after 2 years
• Must apply for SSN when adoption is final
• Can be used to claim child as dependent

sections.title: Preparer Tax Identification Number (PTIN)
sections.content:
**Who Needs a PTIN:**
• ANY person paid to prepare (or help prepare) federal tax returns
• Must be renewed annually
• Required to sign returns as paid preparer

**Format:** Starts with "P" followed by 8 digits

**Annual Renewal:**
• Renewal period: October 1 - December 31
• Annual fee applies
• Must complete PTIN renewal BEFORE preparing returns

**Enrolled Agents:** Must have PTIN in addition to EA credentials

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SSN is required for EIC and refundable credits (work-authorized)
ITIN is for those ineligible for SSN with U.S. tax filing obligation
ITINs start with 9 and have 70-99 as middle digits
EIN is for business entities, estates, trusts, and employers
ATIN is temporary for adopted children pending SSN
PTIN is required for ALL paid preparers - renewed annually
Wrong TIN = rejected e-file, delayed refund, disallowed credits

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals would be *most likely* required to obtain an Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN)?
options: A U.S. citizen employed by a U.S. corporation.
A permanent resident (green card holder) working in the United States.
A nonresident alien with rental income from a property located in the United States.
A U.S. citizen claiming the Earned Income Credit (EIC).
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is a nonresident alien with U.S. rental income. ITINs are issued to individuals who are not eligible for an SSN but are required to file a U.S. tax return or claim a U.S. tax treaty benefit. The other options describe individuals who are generally eligible for and required to obtain an SSN.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which individuals are *not* eligible for a Social Security Number.

reveal.question: What is the primary purpose of Form W-7?
reveal.answer: The primary purpose of Form W-7 is to apply for an Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN) from the IRS. It is used by foreign nationals and other individuals who do not qualify for a Social Security Number (SSN) but need to file a U.S. tax return, claim a tax treaty benefit, or meet other U.S. tax obligations.

rows.label: Eligibility
values: U.S. citizens, permanent residents, and noncitizens authorized to work in the U.S.
Nonresident and resident aliens who do not qualify for an SSN but have a U.S. tax obligation.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Tracking earnings and benefits; required for employment and claiming certain tax credits.
Tax identification for those who cannot obtain an SSN; allows them to comply with U.S. tax laws.

rows.label: Application Form
values: Form SS-5
Form W-7

rows.label: Work Authorization
values: Generally requires work authorization
Does *not* grant work authorization

title: Extensions and Amended Returns
description: Master the rules for filing extensions and correcting previously filed returns

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Extensions and amended returns are daily work for tax practitioners! You MUST know how to properly extend clients by April 15 and when/how to file amended returns to claim refunds or correct errors. Get these wrong, and your client loses money.

sections.title: Form 4868: Automatic Extension
sections.content:
**What Form 4868 Does:**
• Extends filing deadline from April 15 to October 15
• Extension is AUTOMATIC - no approval needed
• Just file by April 15 (or first business day after if on weekend)

**What Form 4868 Does NOT Do:**
• Does NOT extend the time to PAY
• Does NOT stop interest from accruing
• Does NOT prevent failure-to-pay penalty

**How to File:**
• E-file through tax software
• Paper Form 4868
• Credit/debit card payment (acts as extension)
• Electronic Federal Tax Payment System (EFTPS)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Extension ≠ Payment Extension
sections.content:
**The most tested concept about extensions:**

• Form 4868 extends FILING to October 15
• Taxes are still due APRIL 15
• Failure-to-pay penalty: 0.5% per month (max 25%)
• Interest: Federal short-term rate + 3%

**Tip:** Estimate tax and pay with extension to minimize penalties and interest!

sections.title: Form 1040-X: Amended Returns
sections.content:
**When to File 1040-X:**
• Correct errors on filed returns (income, deductions, credits)
• Change filing status
• Claim additional refunds
• Report additional income discovered
• Change itemized ↔ standard deduction

**Do NOT use 1040-X for:**
• Math errors (IRS corrects automatically)
• Missing schedules (they'll request)
• Changing from MFJ to MFS after due date (generally prohibited)

**How to File:**
• Can now e-file most amended returns
• Explain ALL changes in Part III
• Attach supporting schedules

sections.title: Statute of Limitations for Refunds
sections.content:
**To claim a REFUND, you must file within the LATER of:**

**Option 1:** 3 years from the date the original return was FILED

**Option 2:** 2 years from the date the tax was PAID

**If you file within 3 years:** Refund limited to taxes paid within 3 years before claim plus extensions

**If you file within 2 years:** Refund limited to amount paid within 2 years before claim

**After both periods expire:** No refund, even if you overpaid!

sections.title: Example: Refund Limitations
sections.content:
**Scenario:** Tax year 2020 return was due April 15, 2021.

• Filed original return: April 15, 2021
• Paid tax with return: $5,000
• Discoveredoverpayment: $2,000

**Deadline to claim refund:** April 15, 2024 (3 years from filing)

**If claiming on April 10, 2024:** Refund allowed ($2,000)
**If claiming on April 20, 2024:** NO refund allowed - too late!

**Key:** Mark your calendar for the refund claim deadline!

sections.title: Changing Filing Status on Amended Return
sections.content:
**Allowed Changes:**
• Single to HOH (if requirements met)
• MFS to MFJ (if filing timely amended return)
• QSS status correction

**Generally NOT Allowed:**
• MFJ to MFS after due date (including extensions)

**Exception:** MFJ to MFS allowed if:
• Filed before due date, OR
• Court-ordered pursuant to deficiency notice

**Remember:** MFJ is irrevocable after the due date!

sections.title: Waiting for Original Return Processing
sections.content:
**Best Practice:**

Wait for original return to be fully processed BEFORE filing 1040-X:
• Usually 16-20 weeks for e-file
• Can take longer during peak season
• IRS will process in order received

**Why wait?** Filing 1040-X before original is processed can cause significant delays and confusion.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Form 4868 automatically extends filing to October 15
Payment is still due April 15 - extension doesn't extend payment!
Form 1040-X corrects previously filed returns
Refund claim deadline: later of 3 years from filing or 2 years from payment
MFJ cannot be changed to MFS after the due date
Wait for original return processing before filing amendments
E-filing is now available for most amended returns

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer A files Form 4868 on April 10th, requesting an automatic extension of time to file their individual income tax return. They estimate owing $5,000 in taxes but do not remit any payment with Form 4868. Which of the following is TRUE?
options: Taxpayer A has successfully extended the filing deadline to October 15th, and no penalties will be assessed.
Taxpayer A has successfully extended the filing deadline to October 15th, but will be assessed penalties and interest on the unpaid $5,000 from April 15th.
Taxpayer A's extension request will be denied due to non-payment of the estimated tax liability.
Taxpayer A will only be assessed interest on the unpaid $5,000 from April 15th, but no penalties.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Filing Form 4868 automatically extends the filing deadline. However, it does NOT extend the time to pay. Penalties for failure to pay and interest will accrue from the original due date (April 15th).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember what Form 4868 *doesn't* do.

reveal.question: List three situations where you SHOULD file Form 1040-X.
reveal.answer: 1. To correct errors on filed returns (income, deductions, credits). 2. To change filing status. 3. To claim additional refunds. 4. To report additional income discovered. 5. To change itemized ↔ standard deduction.

knowledgeCheck.question: John filed his 2022 tax return on March 1, 2023. On April 20, 2024, he realized he made a mistake in calculating his itemized deductions and wants to file an amended return to claim a larger deduction. What is the LAST DAY John can file an amended return for 2022?
options: April 15, 2025
April 20, 2025
March 1, 2026
April 15, 2026
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The statute of limitations for filing an amended return to claim a refund is generally the later of three years from when you filed your original return or two years from when you paid the tax. In this case, three years from filing (March 1, 2023) is March 1, 2026, and two years from paying the tax is not specified, but we assume it was on or before the filing deadline (or with the return). Therefore, April 15, 2025 is the last day to file.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the statute of limitations for amended returns. Consider BOTH the three-year and two-year rules.

title: Prior Year Data and Carryovers
description: Master the carryover provisions that transfer tax attributes between years

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Carryovers are FREE MONEY your clients may have forgotten! Prior year losses and excess deductions can offset income for many years. Failing to track and use carryovers is leaving money on the table. As an EA, you must know what carries forward and for how long.

sections.title: Net Operating Loss (NOL) Carryovers
sections.content:
**Post-2017 NOL Rules (TCJA):**

• NO carryback allowed (with exceptions)
• INDEFINITE carryforward period
• Limited to 80% of taxable income per year
• 20% of income always taxable

**Exceptions (Full Carryback Allowed):**
• Farming losses (2 years back)
• Certain insurance company losses

**Ordering:** Apply oldest NOL first (FIFO)

**Track carefully!** Client must have records of NOL amounts and when created.

sections.title: Capital Loss Carryovers
sections.content:
**Rules for Individuals:**

**Annual Limit:** Capital losses can only offset:
• ALL capital gains, PLUS
• Up to $3,000 ($1,500 MFS) of ordinary income

**Excess carries forward indefinitely**

**Character is retained:** Short-term remains short-term, long-term remains long-term

**Ordering for carryovers:**
1. Use short-term carryover against net short-term gain
2. Use long-term carryover against net long-term gain
3. Apply net loss against $3,000 ordinary income

**Death:** Capital loss carryover EXPIRES at death - cannot pass to beneficiaries!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Capital Loss at Death
sections.content:
**Capital loss carryovers do NOT transfer to surviving spouse or estate!**

If taxpayer dies with unused capital loss carryover:
• Cannot be used on final return beyond limits
• Cannot be transferred to surviving spouse on MFJ return
• Cannot be inherited by beneficiaries

**Planning tip:** Use losses before death if possible!

sections.title: Charitable Contribution Carryovers
sections.content:
**When Carryover Occurs:**
• Contributions exceed AGI limitation for the year
• Excess carries forward 5 years

**AGI Limitations (2024):**
• 60% AGI: Cash to public charities
• 30% AGI: Property to public charities, Cash to private foundations
• 20% AGI: Property to private foundations

**Ordering:**
1. Current year contributions first (within limits)
2. Then oldest carryover amounts

**Documentation:** Keep records showing contribution year and type for each carryover.

sections.title: Passive Activity Loss Carryovers
sections.content:
**Passive Losses Cannot Offset:**
• Active income (wages, business)
• Portfolio income (interest, dividends)

**Suspended losses carry forward** until:
• You have passive income to offset, OR
• You dispose of the ENTIRE activity in a taxable transaction

**At Disposition:**
• ALL suspended losses become deductible
• Includes losses from all prior years
• Must be complete disposition to unrelated party

**Real Estate Exception:**
Active participation allows $25,000 loss against active income (phases out $100K-$150K AGI).

sections.title: Investment Interest Carryovers
sections.content:
**Investment Interest Deduction Limited To:**
• Net investment income (interest, dividends, certain gains)

**Excess carries forward indefinitely**

**Can elect** to treat qualified dividends and long-term capital gains as investment income (gives up preferential rates)

**Tracking:** Form 4952 calculates limitation and carryover each year.

sections.title: Prior Year Data Review
sections.content:
**At start of every engagement, check:**

• Prior year NOL carryovers
• Capital loss carryforward
• Charitable contribution carryforward
• Suspended passive losses
• Investment interest carryover
• General business credit carryovers
• Alternative minimum tax credit
• Foreign tax credit carryovers

**Get prior returns!** At least 3 years, preferably more if significant carryovers exist.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NOLs carry forward indefinitely but limited to 80% of taxable income
Capital losses: $3,000 annual limit against ordinary income, rest carries forward
Capital loss carryovers expire at death - cannot be inherited
Charitable carryovers last 5 years - use current year first
Passive losses release in full when entire activity is sold
Always review prior returns for carryovers at the start of engagement
Track the year each carryover was created - affects ordering

knowledgeCheck.question: In 2023, Sarah, a single taxpayer, has taxable income of $100,000 before considering a Net Operating Loss (NOL) carryforward from 2022. The NOL carryforward is $90,000. What is Sarah's taxable income after applying the NOL carryforward?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under TCJA rules, NOL carryforwards are limited to 80% of taxable income. Therefore, Sarah can only use $80,000 (80% of $100,000) of the NOL carryforward. Her taxable income will be $100,000 - $80,000 = $20,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the 80% limitation rule for NOL carryforwards post-2017.

reveal.question: What is the maximum amount of ordinary income an individual taxpayer can offset with capital losses in a given year?
reveal.answer: $3,000 ($1,500 if Married Filing Separately).

rows.label: Carryback
values: No carryback allowed (post-2017, with exceptions for farming and certain insurance losses)
Not Applicable

rows.label: Carryforward
values: Indefinite
Indefinite

rows.label: Limitation on Deduction
values: Limited to 80% of taxable income (post-2017)
Limited to offsetting capital gains plus $3,000 ($1,500 MFS) of ordinary income

rows.label: Character
values: Not Applicable
Character is retained (short-term remains short-term, long-term remains long-term)

title: Deceased Taxpayer Returns
description: Master the filing requirements and special rules for returns of deceased taxpayers

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Death doesn't end tax obligations! As an EA, you'll help grieving families navigate final returns, income in respect of a decedent, and estate matters. These rules are tricky and heavily tested on the SEE.

sections.title: Who Files the Final Return?
sections.content:
**The Personal Representative:**
• Executor named in will, OR
• Administrator appointed by court, OR
• Surviving spouse (for joint return), OR
• Other person in charge of decedent's property

**Signing the Return:**
• Sign your own name
• Add "Filing as surviving spouse" or "Personal Representative"
• Attach Form 1310 if claiming refund (unless surviving spouse on joint return)

**Court Certificate:** Attach court certificate if court-appointed representative.

sections.title: Final Return Basics
rows: MFJ available if married at death

sections.title: Filing Status After Death
sections.content:
**Surviving Spouse Options:**

**Year of Death:**
• Can file MFJ for entire year (if married at death)
• Combine all income (pre and post-death)
• If MFS, only pre-death income on final return

**Year After Death (and 2nd year after):**
• Qualifying Surviving Spouse (QSS) if:
  - Dependent child lives with you
  - You maintained the home
  - You didn't remarry

**After 2 Years:**
• Single or HOH (if qualifying child)

sections.title: Income in Respect of a Decedent (IRD)
sections.content:
**What is IRD?**
Income the decedent earned but hadn't received before death.

**Examples:**
• Unpaid wages
• Unpaid commissions
• IRA/pension distributions
• Installment sale payments not yet received
• Partnership income through date of death
• Dividends declared but not paid

**IRD is Taxable to the Recipient:**
• Estate (if paid to estate)
• Beneficiary (if paid directly)
• NOT on decedent's final return!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: IRD Has NO Step-Up in Basis
sections.content:
**Most inherited property gets a stepped-up basis to FMV at death.**

**BUT NOT IRD!**

IRD items retain their character and are fully taxable to whoever receives them:
• IRA distributions are still taxable
• Unpaid wages are still wage income
• Installment sale gain is still recognized

**IRD Deduction:** If estate tax was paid on the IRD, recipient gets an itemized deduction (not subject to 2% floor).

sections.title: Medical Expenses of Decedent
sections.content:
**Special Rule:**

Medical expenses paid within ONE YEAR after death can be deducted:
• On decedent's final return (as itemized deduction), OR
• On estate income tax return (Form 1041), OR
• As estate tax deduction (Form 706)

**Cannot double-dip!** Must choose one.

**Attach Statement:** If deducting on Form 1040, attach statement that expenses won't be claimed elsewhere.

sections.title: Reporting Income on Final Return
sections.content:
**Include:**
• All income received before death
• Constructively received income (available even if not collected)
• Income from assets through date of death

**Do NOT Include:**
• Income received AFTER death (that's IRD or estate income)
• Income accrued but not paid (IRD)

**Example:**
• Paycheck received March 1 for February work: On final return
• Paycheck earned but not paid until after death: IRD (not on final return)

sections.title: Deductions on Final Return
sections.content:
**Standard Deduction:**
• Full amount (not prorated)
• Age 65+ additional amount if applicable

**Itemized Deductions:**
• Expenses paid before death (or within special periods)
• Property taxes due to date of death
• State income taxes for period through death

**Personal Exemption:**
• Eliminated under TCJA through 2025

**AMT Exemption:**
• Full exemption allowed (not prorated)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Personal representative signs and files the final return
Surviving spouse can file MFJ for year of death
Report income received through date of death only
Income in Respect of Decedent (IRD) is taxed to recipient, not decedent
IRD does NOT get stepped-up basis
Medical expenses paid within 1 year can be on final return
Standard deduction is NOT prorated - full amount allowed
Form 1310 required to claim refund (unless surviving spouse on MFJ)

knowledgeCheck.question: Harold died on March 15, 2024. He was married to Wilma at the time of his death. They have one dependent child, Betty, who lived with Wilma all year. What is the latest year Wilma can file as a Qualifying Surviving Spouse (QSS)?
options: Wilma can never file as QSS
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Wilma can file MFJ for 2024 (year of death). She can file as QSS for the two years following the year of death (2025 and 2026) because she has a dependent child living with her. After that, she would likely file as Head of Household if Betty still qualifies as her dependent.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the rules for Qualifying Surviving Spouse: dependent child and household maintenance.

sections.title: Quick Recall: Income in Respect of a Decedent

reveal.question: Define 'Income in Respect of a Decedent' (IRD) in your own words.
reveal.answer: Income the decedent was entitled to receive but had not received before their death. This income is taxable to the recipient (estate or beneficiary) in the year it is received.

rows.label: Appointment
values: Named in the will
Appointed by the court (if no will or executor can't serve)

rows.label: Primary Duty
values: Carry out the instructions in the will
Manage and distribute the estate according to state law

rows.label: Responsibility for Final Return
values: Responsible for filing the final return of the deceased.
Responsible for filing the final return of the deceased.

title: Community Property Rules
description: Understand how community property laws affect tax filing in applicable states

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: In community property states, income splitting rules are MANDATORY for MFS filers. This can significantly affect tax liability, especially for spouses with disparate incomes. The SEE tests this because many practitioners overlook these critical rules.

sections.title: Community Property States
sections.content:
**Nine Community Property States:**

• **A**rizona
• **C**alifornia
• **I**daho
• **L**ouisiana
• **N**evada
• **N**ew Mexico
• **T**exas
• **W**ashington
• **W**isconsin

**Also:** Alaska, South Dakota, and Tennessee allow opt-in community property.

**Memory aid:** "All Community Income Laws Need New Taxation, Whichever Works."

**Puerto Rico** also has community property rules.

sections.title: Community vs. Separate Property
rows: Wages, business income, property bought during marriage
Property owned before marriage OR gifts/inheritance to one spouse
Pre-marriage assets, inheritance, personal injury awards

sections.title: Income Splitting for MFS Filers
sections.content:
**When Filing Married Filing Separately in a CP State:**

Each spouse reports:
• 50% of community income
• 100% of their own separate income

**Community Income Includes:**
• Wages earned during marriage (both spouses' wages!)
• Income from community property
• Business income from community business

**Separate Income Includes:**
• Income from separate property
• Income earned before marriage

sections.title: Example: MFS in Community Property State
sections.content:
**Spouse A:** Earns $100,000 salary
**Spouse B:** Earns $20,000 salary
**Total Community Income:** $120,000

**If Filing MFS, Each Reports:**
• Spouse A: $60,000 (half of $120,000)
• Spouse B: $60,000 (half of $120,000)

**NOT:**
• Spouse A: $100,000
• Spouse B: $20,000

**Result:** Income is equalized between spouses!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Earned Income Credit
sections.content:
**Community property rules can affect EIC!**

Each spouse reports half the community earned income.

This could:
• Reduce EIC if high earner's income is now shared
• Increase EIC if low earner now has more income

**But watch out:** Investment income also must be split - could push over $11,600 limit!

**Some states have special EIC rules:**
California and Washington allow non-earning spouse to treat other spouse's income as their own for EIC.

sections.title: Separate Property Exceptions
sections.content:
**Property Remains Separate When:**

1. **Acquired before marriage**
2. **Received as gift** to one spouse only
3. **Inherited** by one spouse
4. **Personal injury awards** (in most states)
5. **Property exchanged** for separate property

**Income from Separate Property:**
• Most states: Remains separate
• Some states (Texas, Idaho): Becomes community income

**Tracing:** Must be able to prove property remained separate.

sections.title: Special Rules for Registered Domestic Partners
sections.content:
**In States Recognizing RDPs:**

Registered domestic partners in community property states:
• Must follow community property rules for state returns
• May have complex federal treatment (varies by state law)

**California, Nevada, Washington:** RDPs must split income on state MFS returns (if state allows MFS).

sections.title: When Living in Different States
sections.content:
**Domicile Determines Rules:**

• Community property rules apply if DOMICILED in a CP state
• Moving from CP to non-CP state: Property retains CP character
• Moving from non-CP to CP state: Property retains separate character

**"Quasi-Community Property":**
Some states treat property that WOULD have been CP (if acquired in CP state) as CP for divorce/death purposes.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 9 community property states: AZ, CA, ID, LA, NV, NM, TX, WA, WI
When filing MFS, each spouse reports 50% of community income
Wages earned during marriage are community income
Gifts and inheritance remain separate property
Income from separate property treatment varies by state
Community property rules can affect EIC eligibility
Domicile determines which state's rules apply

knowledgeCheck.question: John and Mary reside in Texas, a community property state. John earns $80,000 in wages, and Mary inherits $50,000 from her deceased grandmother. They file Married Filing Separately. How much must Mary report as income?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Mary must report 100% of her separate income (the inheritance, which is considered separate property) which is $50,000. John's wages are community property, but Mary only reports her share of the community income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the rules for separate vs. community property and MFS filing.

reveal.question: List the nine community property states.
reveal.answer: Arizona, California, Idaho, Louisiana, Nevada, New Mexico, Texas, Washington, and Wisconsin. (ACILNNTWW)

knowledgeCheck.question: A couple living in California owns a business acquired during their marriage. The business generated $100,000 in net income. If they file Married Filing Separately, how much income must each spouse report from the business, assuming no separate property contributions?
options: Spouse A: $0, Spouse B: $100,000
Spouse A: $100,000, Spouse B: $0
Each spouse: $50,000
It depends on who actively manages the business.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: In a community property state, income from a business acquired during the marriage is considered community income. When filing separately, each spouse reports 50% of the community income, regardless of who actively manages the business.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on how community income is treated when filing separately.

title: Wages, Salaries, and Tips
description: Understand the taxation of employment income and common reporting issues

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: W-2 income is the most common form of taxable income. Understanding what's included, what's excluded, and how to properly report it is fundamental. The SEE tests various scenarios involving wages, tips, and fringe benefits.

sections.title: Form W-2 Basics
sections.content:
**Key Boxes on Form W-2:**

• **Box 1:** Wages, tips, other compensation (taxable income)
• **Box 2:** Federal income tax withheld
• **Box 3:** Social Security wages
• **Box 4:** Social Security tax withheld
• **Box 5:** Medicare wages and tips
• **Box 6:** Medicare tax withheld
• **Box 12:** Various codes for benefits
• **Box 13:** Statutory employee, retirement plan, third-party sick pay

**Why Box 1 ≠ Box 3 or Box 5:**
Pre-tax deductions (401k, health insurance, etc.) reduce Box 1 but not always Social Security wages.

sections.title: Box 12 Codes You Need to Know
Tip Income
sections.content:
**All tips are taxable income:**
• Cash tips
• Credit card tips
• Tips shared with other employees
• Non-cash tips (fair market value)

**Reporting Requirements:**
• Tips of $20+ in a month MUST be reported to employer
• Report by 10th of following month
• Employer withholds income and FICA taxes
• Unreported tips → Form 4137 with return

**Allocated Tips (Box 8):**
If tips seem low, employer may allocate additional tips. These are NOT included in Box 1 - taxpayer must report as additional income if not already reported.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Allocated Tips
sections.content:
**Box 8 - Allocated Tips:**

• NOT included in Box 1 wages
• NOT automatically subject to withholding
• Employee MUST include as income if tips weren't already reported
• If you reported all tips, allocated tips may be disputed

**Key:** Allocated tips are a RED FLAG for underreporting!

sections.title: Taxable vs. Nontaxable Fringe Benefits
rows: Small value, administratively impractical to account
Would be deductible if paid by employee

sections.title: Statutory Employees
sections.content:
**Statutory Employees (Box 13 checked):**

Treated as employees for FICA but self-employed for income tax:
• Life insurance salespersons
• Certain agent-drivers or commission drivers
• Traveling salespersons
• Home workers (some)

**Special Treatment:**
• Report income and expenses on Schedule C
• Deduct business expenses (even without itemizing)
• Do NOT pay self-employment tax (already paid FICA)

**Key:** Look for the statutory employee checkbox on W-2!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Box 1 on W-2 shows taxable wages after pre-tax deductions
All tips are taxable - cash, credit card, shared, and non-cash
Tips $20+/month must be reported to employer by the 10th
Allocated tips (Box 8) may need to be added to income
Group-term life insurance over $50,000 = taxable benefit
Box 12 codes show various pre-tax and informational amounts
Statutory employees file Schedule C but don't pay SE tax

knowledgeCheck.question: An employee's Form W-2 shows $60,000 in Box 1 (Wages, tips, other compensation), $3,720 in Box 4 (Social Security tax withheld), and $870 in Box 6 (Medicare tax withheld). The employee received $5,000 in tips during the year but only reported $3,000 to the employer. What is the amount of unreported tips subject to Social Security and Medicare taxes?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The employee failed to report $2,000 in tips ($5,000 - $3,000). This unreported amount is subject to Social Security and Medicare taxes. The amount already reported to the employer and reflected on the W-2 is not the focus of this question.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the difference between the total tips received and the amount reported to the employer.

reveal.question: What is the minimum amount of tips an employee must receive in a month to be required to report them to their employer?
reveal.answer: An employee must report tips to their employer if they receive $20 or more in tips during a month.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following fringe benefits is generally NOT included as taxable wages on Form W-2?
options: Use of a company car for personal purposes
Group-term life insurance coverage over $50,000
Qualified retirement plan contributions made by the employer
Cash bonuses
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Qualified retirement plan contributions made by the employer are generally excluded from taxable wages. The other options are typically considered taxable fringe benefits and would be included on Form W-2.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what benefits are typically tax-deferred.

title: Capital Gains and Losses
description: Master the rules for taxing gains and losses on capital assets

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Capital gains and losses are among the most complex areas of individual taxation!The SEE heavily tests holding periods, netting rules, and rate structures. Master this and you'll excel on many exam questions.

sections.title: What is a Capital Asset?
sections.content:
**Capital Asset = Everything EXCEPT (what it's NOT):**

• Inventory or property held for sale to customers
• Depreciable property used in business (Section 1231)
• Real property used in business (Section 1231)
• Accounts/notes receivable from business
• Supplies used in business
• Certain intellectual property (copyrights, etc.)
• Government publications

**Examples of Capital Assets:**
• Stocks and bonds
• Personal residence
• Personal-use property (car, furniture)
• Investment real estate (not used in business)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Capital Asset Exclusions
sections.content:
**"ACID"** is NOT a capital asset:

**A**ccounts receivable (from services or inventory)
**C**opyrights and creative works (in creator's/donee's hands)
**I**nventory and dealer property
**D**epreciable property/real estate used in trade or business

sections.title: Holding Period
sections.content:
**Short-Term:** Held 1 year or LESS
• Taxed as ordinary income (up to 37%)

**Long-Term:** Held MORE than 1 year
• Taxed at preferential rates (0%, 15%, or 20%)

**How to Count:**
• Start the day AFTER acquisition
• End on the day of sale
• "More than 1 year" = 1 year + 1 day minimum

**Example:** Buy March 15, 2024 → Must sell after March 15, 2025 for LTCG

sections.title: Capital Gains Netting Process
sections.content:
**Step 1: Net within each category**
• Short-term gains against short-term losses → Net ST gain or loss
• Long-term gains against long-term losses → Net LT gain or loss

**Step 2: Net between categories (if applicable)**
• Net ST gain + Net LT loss = Combined result
• Net ST loss + Net LT gain = Combined result

**Step 3: Apply final result**
• Net capital gain → Taxed at applicable rates
• Net capital loss → Deduct up to $3,000 vs. ordinary income

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: $3,000 Loss Limitation
sections.content:
**Capital Losses:**

• First offset capital gains
• Then deduct up to $3,000 against ordinary income ($1,500 MFS)
• Excess carries forward indefinitely
• Carryover keeps its character (ST or LT)

**Example:**
• $10,000 capital loss
• $2,000 capital gain
• Net loss: $8,000
• Current year deduction: $3,000
• Carryforward to next year: $5,000

sections.title: Special Capital Gains Rates
rows: On top of regular rates for high earners

sections.title: Wash Sale Rule
sections.content:
**The Wash Sale Rule Disallows Losses When:**

You sell stock at a loss AND buy substantially identical stock within:
• 30 days BEFORE the sale, OR
• 30 days AFTER the sale

**Result:** Loss is disallowed and added to the basis of the replacement stock.

**Wash sale period:** 61 days total (30 before + sale date + 30 after)

**Does NOT apply to gains!**

**Example:** Sell 100 XYZ at $1,000 loss on March 15. Buy 100 XYZ on March 28. Loss disallowed → added to new shares' basis.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capital assets = everything EXCEPT inventory, business property, A/R, etc.
Holding period: LTCG requires more than 1 year (1 year + 1 day)
LTCG rates: 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on taxable income
Netting: ST vs ST, LT vs LT, then across categories
Capital loss deduction limited to $3,000 per year ($1,500 MFS)
Excess losses carry forward indefinitely
Wash sale rule disallows losses if repurchase within 30 days before/after

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following assets would NOT be considered a capital asset for tax purposes?
options: A personal automobile held for family use.
Shares of stock held as an investment.
Land held for investment purposes.
Inventory held by a retail business for sale to customers.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Inventory held for sale to customers is specifically excluded from the definition of a capital asset. Capital assets are generally personal-use property or investment property, not assets held in the ordinary course of business.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider what the asset is used for - personal use, investment, or business operations.

reveal.question: How is the holding period for a capital asset calculated to determine if it is short-term or long-term?
reveal.answer: The holding period starts the day AFTER acquisition and ends on the day of sale. Short-term is held for 1 year or less. Long-term is held for more than 1 year (1 year + 1 day or more).

knowledgeCheck.question: An individual purchased stock on July 15, 2022, and sold it on July 15, 2023. Is the gain or loss from the sale considered short-term or long-term?
options: Short-term
Long-term
It depends on the taxpayer's marginal tax rate
The holding period is exactly one year, so it is neither short-term nor long-term.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Because the stock was held exactly one year, it does not meet the 'more than one year' requirement for long-term capital gains treatment. The holding period begins the day *after* acquisition and ends on the day of sale. Therefore, it's a short-term gain.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the rule about 'more than one year' and when the holding period starts.

title: Qualified Business Income Deduction
description: Master Section 199A and the 20% pass-through deduction rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Section 199A QBI deduction can save clients up to 20% on pass-through business income! It's also one of the most complex provisions in the tax code. The SEE tests this heavily because it's so important for tax practitioners serving small businesses.

sections.title: What is QBI?
sections.content:
**Qualified Business Income (QBI):**

The net amount of qualified items of income, gain, deduction, and loss from any qualified trade or business.

**Includes:**
• Schedule C income (sole proprietors)
• S corporation income (K-1)
• Partnership income (K-1)
• REIT dividends and PTP income

**Does NOT Include:**
• W-2 wages
• Capital gains/losses
• Interest income not properly allocable
• Reasonable compensation from S-corp
• Guaranteed payments from partnership

sections.title: Basic QBI Deduction
sections.content:
**For Taxpayers Below Thresholds:**

Deduction = **20% of QBI**

**2024 Thresholds:**
• Single/HOH: $182,100 taxable income
• MFJ: $364,200 taxable income

**Below threshold = No limitations!**
• SSTB rules don't apply
• W-2 wage/property limitations don't apply
• Simply 20% of QBI

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The QBI Calculation
sections.content:
**QBI Deduction = LESSER of:**

**Option A:** 20% of QBI from all qualifying businesses

**Option B:** 20% of (Taxable Income − Net Capital Gains)

**Then apply W-2 and SSTB limitations if over threshold!**

sections.title: Specified Service Trade or Business (SSTB)
sections.content:
**SSTBs are penalized at higher income levels!**

**SSTB Fields:**
• Health (doctors, dentists, nurses, etc.)
• Law (attorneys)
• Accounting
• Actuarial science
• Performing arts
• Consulting
• Athletics
• Financial services
• Brokerage services
• Any trade where principal asset is reputation/skill of employees

**Specifically EXCLUDED from SSTB:**
• Engineering
• Architecture
• Real estate agents/brokers (if otherwise qualifying)

sections.title: W-2 Wage and Property Limitation
sections.content:
**For Taxpayers ABOVE Thresholds:**

QBI deduction limited to the GREATER of:

**Option 1:** 50% of W-2 wages paid by the business

**Option 2:** 25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of unadjusted basis of qualified property

**Qualified Property:**
• Depreciable tangible property
• Held at close of tax year
• Used in production of QBI
• Within depreciable period (greater of 10 years or MACRS period)

**Phase-in:** Applies fully above threshold; phases in through phase-out range.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Aggregation Rules
sections.content:
**Aggregating Businesses:**

Taxpayers can aggregate multiple qualifying businesses if:
• Same owners (within tolerance)
• Common ownership between businesses
• Businesses share resources (employees, accounting, etc.)

**Why aggregate?**
• W-2 wages from one business can cover QBI from another
• Qualified property from one business helps another
• Can't cherry-pick which to aggregate each year

**Key:** Once aggregated, must continue in future years.

sections.title: Example: QBI Deduction Calculation
sections.content:
**Facts:**
• Sarah (single) has $300,000 taxable income
• Schedule C net income: $200,000 (consulting = SSTB)
• No W-2 wages paid, no qualified property

**Analysis:**
• Above $232,100 threshold → SSTB gets NO deduction
• If she were at $182,000 TI → Full 20% deduction ($40,000)
• If she were at $207,100 TI → Partial deduction (50% of otherwise allowable)

**Key:** For high-income SSTBs, converting to C-corp may be beneficial!

sections.title: TCJA Sunset - 2026
sections.content:
**Section 199A is scheduled to expire after December 31, 2025!**

Unless extended by Congress:
• No QBI deduction for 2026 and beyond
• Significant tax increase for pass-through businesses
• May impact entity choice decisions

**EA exam implication:** Know the current rules, but be aware of the sunset.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: QBI deduction = up to 20% of qualified business income
Below threshold: simple 20% deduction, no limitations
SSTB deduction phases out and disappears at high incomes
W-2 wage and property limitations apply above thresholds
SSTB includes health, law, accounting, consulting, financial services
Engineering and architecture are NOT SSTBs
Section 199A is scheduled to expire after 2025

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following items is NOT considered when calculating Qualified Business Income (QBI)?
options: Schedule C income from a sole proprietorship.
Capital gains from the sale of stock.
S corporation income reported on Schedule K-1.
Partnership income reported on Schedule K-1.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Capital gains are not considered qualified business income. QBI is derived from the ordinary income of a qualified trade or business. Capital gains are taxed separately.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the nature of QBI - it comes from operating a business.

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer Smith, filing as Single, has taxable income of $170,000 and QBI of $80,000 from his accounting practice. What is the amount of his Section 199A deduction?
options: $0, as accounting is an SSTB.
The deduction cannot be determined without knowing the W-2 wages paid.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since Smith's taxable income ($170,000) is below the threshold for Single filers ($182,100), the SSTB rules do not apply, and the deduction is simply 20% of QBI: 20% * $80,000 = $16,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to check if the taxable income is below the threshold before considering SSTB rules.

reveal.question: What are the 2024 taxable income thresholds for the QBI deduction for Single filers and Married Filing Jointly?
reveal.answer: Single/HOH: $182,100. MFJ: $364,200. Taxable income above these thresholds may trigger limitations based on W-2 wages, property, and SSTB status.

title: Earned Income Credit
description: Master the complex EIC rules and due diligence requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The EIC is one of the largest anti-poverty programs in the U.S. and is HEAVILY tested on the SEE! It's also a major audit area with strict due diligence requirements for preparers. Get this wrong and you face penalties. Get it right and you change lives.

sections.title: EIC Overview
sections.content:
**What is the EIC?**
A refundable credit for low-to-moderate income workers, especially those with qualifying children.

**Key Characteristics:**
• REFUNDABLE - can get money even with zero tax liability
• Phases in with income, then phases out
• Credit amount depends on number of qualifying children
• Available to workers without children (smaller credit)

**Maximum Credits (2024):**
• No children: $632
• 1 child: $4,213
• 2 children: $6,960
• 3+ children: $7,830

sections.title: Basic EIC Requirements
rows: Must have wages, SE income, or other earned income
Valid SSN for taxpayer, spouse, and qualifying children
Cannot be claimed as dependent on another return
Also can't be a qualifying child of another
Main home in U.S. for more than half the year

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: EIC Requirements
sections.content:
**"RESIDE":**

**R**equires earned income
**E**arnings under phase-out limit
**S**SN required (valid for work)
**I**nvestment income under $11,600
**D**ependency - can't be claimed by others
**E**xclude MFS status

sections.title: EIC Qualifying Child Rules
sections.content:
**Different from Dependency Qualifying Child!**

**Relationship:** Child, stepchild, foster child, sibling, or descendant

**Age:** Under 19 (or under 24 if student, or any age if disabled)

**Residency:** Lived with taxpayer for MORE than half the year

**Joint return:** Child cannot file MFJ (unless only for refund)

**NO support test!** (Unlike dependency rules)

**Key Difference:** No gross income test, no support test for EIC!

sections.title: ⚠️ Investment Income Limit
sections.content:
**Investment Income Must Be ≤ $11,600 (2024)**

**Investment income includes:**
• Taxable interest
• Tax-exempt interest
• Ordinary dividends
• Capital gain net income
• Net rental/royalty income (if positive)
• Passive income

**This is an absolute limit - exceed it and EIC = $0!**

sections.title: Preparer Due Diligence - Form 8867
sections.content:
**Paid preparers MUST complete Form 8867 for claims including:**
• Earned Income Credit
• Child Tax Credit / ACTC
• American Opportunity Credit
• Head of Household status

**Due Diligence Requirements:**
1. Complete eligibility checklist on Form 8867
2. Compute credits using Form 8867
3. Verify documentation of proof
4. Know Rules - apply knowledge to specific return

**Penalty for failure: $635 per failure (2024)**

A preparation with all 4 credits = potential $2,540 penalty per return!

sections.title: Documentation Preparers Should Obtain
content: School records showing child's address
Landlord or property management statements
Healthcare provider records
Social service agency records
Employer records
Day care records
Documents showing relationship (birth certificates)
Keep records for 3 years from due date or date filed
items: **Identification**: Driver's license or state ID for taxpayer and spouse.
**SSN Cards**: Social Security cards for all individuals on return.
**Income Statements**: W-2s, 1099s (INT, DIV, NEC, MISC, R, etc.), K-1s.
**Expense Proof**: Receipts needed for itemized deductions or credits (child care, education).
**Health/Marketplace**: Form 1095-A if Marketplace insurance used.
**Prior Returns**: Copy of last year's tax return.

sections.title: Common EIC Issues and Audits
sections.content:
**IRS Common Audit Triggers:**

• Claiming child who lives with someone else
• Filing status errors (HOH when shouldn't)
• Income discrepancies
• Child SSN issues
• Multiple filers claiming same child

**Most common EIC error:** Claiming a child who doesn't meet residency requirement!

**Result of erroneous claim:**
• Credit recapture + interest
• 2-year ban (reckless or intentional disregard)
• 10-year ban (fraud)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: EIC is a refundable credit for low-to-moderate income workers
Maximum credit with 3+ children is $7,830 (2024)
Investment income limit: $11,600 (absolute cutoff)
Must have valid work-authorized SSN
Cannot file MFS to claim EIC
Qualifying child rules differ from dependency rules (no support test)
Form 8867 due diligence required - $635 penalty per failure
Keep documentation records for 3 years

sections.title: EIC Qualifying Child Rules - Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals CANNOT be claimed as a qualifying child for the Earned Income Credit (EIC)?
options: A 17-year-old child who lived with the taxpayer all year and had $2,000 in unearned income.
A 22-year-old full-time student who lived with the taxpayer all year and is claimed as a dependent.
A 10-year-old foster child who lived with the taxpayer for 8 months of the year.
A 28-year-old disabled adult child who lived with the taxpayer all year and is claimed as a dependent.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The age test for a qualifying child generally requires the child to be under age 19, or under age 24 if a student. There is no age limit if the child is permanently and totally disabled. However, if the child is over age 23 AND not disabled, they cannot be a qualifying child.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the age and disability requirements for a qualifying child.

reveal.question: What does it mean for the Earned Income Credit (EIC) to be a 'refundable' credit?
reveal.answer: A refundable credit means that a taxpayer can receive a refund even if the credit amount exceeds their tax liability. In other words, they can receive money back from the government even if they didn't owe any taxes.

sections.title: EIC and Dependency: Qualifying Child Comparison

rows.label: Relationship
values: Child, stepchild, foster child, sibling, or descendant
Child, stepchild, foster child, sibling, half-sibling, stepsibling, or descendant of any of them

rows.label: Residency
values: Lived with taxpayer for MORE than half the year
Lived with taxpayer for MORE than half the year

rows.label: Joint Return
values: Child cannot file MFJ (unless only to claim a refund)
Child cannot file MFJ (unless only to claim a refund)

rows.label: Age
values: Under 19 (or under 24 if student, or any age if disabled)
Under 19 (or under 24 if student)

rows.label: Citizenship
values: U.S. citizen or U.S. resident alien
U.S. citizen, U.S. national, or resident of U.S., Canada, or Mexico

title: Interest Income
description: Understand all types of interest income and their tax treatment

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Interest income comes in many forms with different tax treatments. Know when it's taxable, when it's tax-exempt, and how to properly report it. This is foundational for the SEE!

sections.title: Taxable vs. Tax-Exempt Interest
rows: Federal exempt, may be State taxable

sections.title: Original Issue Discount (OID)
sections.content:
**What is OID?**
The difference between a bond's stated redemption price at maturity and its original issue price.

**How it's taxed:**
• Treated as interest income over the life of the bond
• Must include in income each year, even if not received
• Reported on Form 1099-OID

**Example:**
• Buy bond for $900, matures at $1,000
• OID = $100 spread over holding period
• Include portion as interest income annually

sections.title: Series EE and I Savings Bonds
sections.content:
**General Rule:**
Interest is taxable when the bond is redeemed (cash method).

**Election to report annually:**
• Can elect to report interest each year
• Once elected, applies to all bonds and future bonds

**Education Exclusion (Series EE/I):**
• Can exclude interest if used for qualified education expenses
• Must be purchased when owner is 24+
• Income limitations apply
• Must be used for tuition and fees (not room/board)

sections.title: Nominee Interest
sections.content:
**What is Nominee Interest?**
Interest reported to you on Form 1099-INT that actually belongs to someone else.

**How to handle:**
1. Include the full amount on Schedule B
2. Subtract nominee portion with "NOMINEE" notation
3. Issue Form 1099-INT to the actual owner (if $10+)

**Common situations:**
• Joint accounts where other person is beneficial owner
• Custodial accounts

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Bank and CD interest is fully taxable
Treasury bond interest is federal taxable but state exempt
Municipal bond interest is federal exempt
OID must be included in income annually as it accrues
Series EE/I bond interest can be deferred until redemption
Education exclusion available for EE/I bonds used for tuition
Nominee interest must be reported and then subtracted

title: Dividend Income
description: Master the taxation of ordinary and qualified dividends

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Dividends are taxed at different rates depending on their classification. Knowing the difference between qualified and ordinary dividends can significantly impact your client's tax liability.

sections.title: Types of Dividends
rows: Holding period met, from domestic corp or qualified foreign corp
All dividends that don't qualify for preferential rates
From mutual funds, represents fund's LTCG

sections.title: Qualified Dividend Requirements
sections.content:
**To qualify for preferential rates:**

**1. Holding Period:**
• Must hold stock for more than 60 days during 121-day period
• Period starts 60 days before ex-dividend date

**2. Type of Stock:**
• Domestic corporation stock
• Qualified foreign corporation stock
• NOT dividends from tax-exempt organizations
• NOT dividends from REITs (generally)

**Qualified dividends appear in Box 1b of Form 1099-DIV**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: REIT and Mutual Fund Dividends
sections.content:
**REIT Dividends:**
• Generally NOT qualified dividends
• Taxed as ordinary income
• Exception: Capital gain distributions from REIT

**Mutual Fund Dividends:**
• Can be qualified if underlying stocks meet requirements
• Capital gain distributions are always LTCG rate
• Look at 1099-DIV boxes carefully!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Qualified dividends taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20% rates
Must hold stock 60+ days in 121-day window
REIT dividends are generally ordinary income
Capital gain distributions get LTCG treatment
Return of capital reduces basis, not immediately taxable

knowledgeCheck.question: An individual purchased stock in a domestic corporation on March 1st. The ex-dividend date for the stock was May 15th. To qualify for the preferential tax rate on the dividend, the individual must hold the stock for more than 60 days during the 121-day period that begins:
options: January 15th.
March 16th.
March 1st.
May 15th.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The 121-day period begins 60 days before the ex-dividend date. Therefore, it begins on March 16th. The taxpayer must hold the stock for more than 60 days within this 121-day period to qualify for the preferential rate.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the 60-day before ex-dividend date rule.

reveal.question: What are the two primary requirements for a dividend to be considered a qualified dividend?
reveal.answer:
1. **Holding Period:** The stock must be held for more than 60 days during the 121-day period starting 60 days before the ex-dividend date.
2. **Type of Stock:** The dividend must be from a domestic corporation or a qualified foreign corporation (and not from tax-exempt entities).

rows.label: Tax Rate
values: Taxed at the taxpayer's ordinary income tax rate.
Taxed at preferential capital gains rates (0%, 15%, or 20% depending on the taxpayer's income).

rows.label: Holding Period Requirement
values: No specific holding period requirement.
Must meet the holding period requirement (more than 60 days during the 121-day period starting 60 days before the ex-dividend date).

title: Rental Income and Expenses
description: Learn to properly report rental income and deductible expenses on Schedule E

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Rental real estate is one of the most common investments. Understanding how to properly report income and expenses, handle mixed-use situations, and navigate passive activity rules is essential for serving clients.

sections.title: Rental Income
sections.content:
**What is Rental Income?**
Any payment received for the use or occupation of property.

**Includes:**
• Rent payments
• Advance rent (taxable when received)
• Security deposits kept
• Tenant-paid expenses (fair market value)
• Cancellation payments

**Does NOT include:**
• Refundable security deposits (until kept)
• Tenant improvements in lieu of rent (special rules)

sections.title: Deductible Rental Expenses
rows: On Schedule E, not Schedule A

sections.title: Depreciation of Rental Property
sections.content:
**Residential Rental Property:**
• 27.5 years straight-line
• Mid-month convention

**Nonresidential Rental Property:**
• 39 years straight-line
• Mid-month convention

**Land is never depreciable!**

**Basis for depreciation:**
• Cost + improvements - land value
• Allocate purchase price between land and building

sections.title: Personal Use Days Test
sections.content:
**The "14-Day" or "10%" Rules:**

**Minimal personal use (true rental):**
• Personal use ≤ GREATER of 14 days or 10% of rental days
• Full rental expense deductions allowed
• Passive activity rules apply

**Dwelling unit as residence:**
• Personal use > GREATER of 14 days or 10% of rental days
• Property is treated as residence
• Rental expenses limited to rental income
• No loss allowed to offset other income

**Personal use = Used by owner, family, or anyone paying less than fair rent**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Vacation Home Rules
sections.content:
**Special Scenario: Rented AND Personal Use**

If property rented < 15 days AND used personally:
• Rental income is TAX-FREE (not reported!)
• No rental expenses deductible
• Mortgage interest and taxes may still be deductible on Schedule A

**This is the famous "Augusta Rule" - commonly tested!**

sections.title: Passive Activity Loss Rules
sections.content:
**Rental activities are generally passive!**

• Losses limited to passive income
• Excess losses suspended and carried forward

**$25,000 Exception:**
• Can deduct up to $25,000 of rental losses against nonpassive income
• Must actively participate (make management decisions)
• Phases out: AGI $100,000 - $150,000
• Completely gone at $150,000 AGI

**Real Estate Professional Exception:**
• More than 50% of services in real property trades
• 750+ hours of material participation
• All rental activities can be treated as nonpassive

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Advance rent is taxable when received, not when earned
Security deposits are income only when kept
Residential rental property depreciates over 27.5 years
Personal use > 14 days or 10% = treated as residence
Rented < 15 days = income tax-free, no expense deduction
$25,000 rental loss exception phases out $100K-$150K AGI
Real estate professionals can deduct rental losses fully

knowledgeCheck.question: John owns a rental property.
