In the current year, he received $12,000 in rent payments, kept a $500 security deposit due to damages, and received $300 from a tenant to cover a repair that was John's responsibility. What amount should John include as rental income on Schedule E?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: John must include all rent payments ($12,000), the security deposit kept ($500), and the tenant-paid expenses for repairs that were John's responsibility ($300). Therefore, the total rental income is $12,000 + $500 + $300 = $12,800.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to include all forms of payment received for the use of the property, including those covering expenses normally paid by the landlord.

reveal.question: What is the recovery period for residential rental property depreciation?
reveal.answer: 27.5 years using the straight-line method and the mid-month convention.

rows.label: Personal Use Threshold
values: ≤ GREATER of 14 days or 10% of rental days
> GREATER of 14 days or 10% of rental days

rows.label: Expense Deduction
values: Full rental expense deductions allowed (subject to passive activity rules)
Expenses limited to gross rental income; no loss allowed

rows.label: Passive Activity Rules
values: Passive activity rules apply
Passive activity rules apply (but deductions limited)

title: Business Income (Schedule C)
description: Master sole proprietorship income reporting and common business deductions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Schedule C is one of the most common business forms you'll prepare! Sole proprietors report business income and expenses here. Understanding what's deductible, what's not, and how to calculate net profit is foundational for every tax practitioner.

sections.title: Schedule C Overview
sections.content:
**Who Files Schedule C?**
• Sole proprietors (single-member LLCs by default)
• Independent contractors
• Gig workers (Uber, DoorDash, freelancers)
• Anyone with self-employment income

**Key Sections:**
• Part I: Income (gross receipts, returns, COGS)
• Part II: Expenses (ordinary and necessary business expenses)
• Part III: Cost of Goods Sold (if applicable)
• Part IV: Vehicle Information (if claiming vehicle expenses)
• Part V: Other Expenses (with descriptions)

sections.title: Gross Receipts and Income
sections.content:
**Report ALL business income:**

• Cash payments
• Checks received
• Credit card payments
• 1099-NEC amounts
• 1099-K amounts (payment apps, credit cards)
• Bartering income (at FMV)
• Cancelled business debt

**Constructive Receipt:**
Income is taxable when available to you, even if not deposited yet.

**Returns and Allowances:**
Reduce gross receipts by refunds given to customers.

sections.title: Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)
sections.content:
**For businesses that sell products:**

**Formula:**
Beginning Inventory
+ Purchases
+ Labor (direct manufacturing)
+ Materials and Supplies
+ Other costs
= Cost of Goods Available
- Ending Inventory
= **COGS**

**Inventory Methods:**
• FIFO (First-In, First-Out) - most common
• LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) - election required
• Specific identification
• Average cost

**Service businesses** generally don't have COGS.

sections.title: Common Deductible Expenses
rows: Business loans, credit cards for business

sections.title: Home Office Deduction
sections.content:
**Two Methods:**

**1. Simplified Method:**
• $5 per square foot of home office
• Maximum 300 square feet
• Maximum deduction: $1,500
• No depreciation, no carryover

**2. Regular Method:**
• Calculate business percentage of home (sq ft or room count)
• Apply % to: mortgage interest, property taxes, insurance, utilities, depreciation
• Can create carryforward if limited by income

**Both require:**
• Exclusive use (used ONLY for business)
• Regular use (consistent, not occasional)
• Principal place of business OR place to meet clients

sections.title: Vehicle Expense Methods
sections.content:
**Standard Mileage Rate (2024):**
• 67 cents per business mile
• Add: parking fees, tolls
• Must use in first year vehicle is placed in service to continue using

**Actual Expense Method:**
• Track all costs: gas, insurance, repairs, depreciation
• Multiply by business use percentage
• More record-keeping but may be higher deduction

**Cannot switch freely:** If you start with standard mileage, you can switch to actual. If you start with actual using MACRS depreciation, you cannot switch to standard mileage.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Self-Employment Tax
sections.content:
**Net profit from Schedule C is subject to SE tax!**

• Social Security: 12.4% (up to $168,600 in 2024)
• Medicare: 2.9% (no limit)
• Additional Medicare: 0.9% (over $200K/$250K MFJ)

**Total: 15.3%** (up to SS limit)

**Deduction:** 50% of SE tax is an above-the-line deduction (Schedule 1)

**Minimum:** SE tax applies if net earnings ≥ $400

sections.title: Ordinary and Necessary Standard
sections.content:
**All business expenses must be:**

**Ordinary:** Common and accepted in your trade or business

**Necessary:** Helpful and appropriate for your business

**Personal expenses are NEVER deductible:**
• Commuting to regular job location
• Personal clothing (even if worn at work)
• Personal meals (unless travel or business purpose)
• Personal portion of home expenses

**Mixed use:** Must allocate between personal and business

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Schedule C reports sole proprietorship income and expenses
Gross receipts include ALL income, including barter at FMV
COGS calculation for businesses selling products
Standard mileage: 67¢/mile (2024), or use actual expenses
Home office: simplified ($5/sq ft, max $1,500) or regular method
Expenses must be ordinary and necessary for the business
Net profit subject to 15.3% SE tax (plus deduction for half)
SE tax applies if net earnings ≥ $400

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals is LEAST likely to file Schedule C?
options: A self-employed graphic designer who receives payments directly from clients.
A real estate agent working as an independent contractor.
An employee receiving a W-2 from their employer.
A rideshare driver for a company like Uber.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Employees receiving a W-2 report their income on Form 1040, not Schedule C. Schedule C is for self-employment income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the definition of self-employment.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)?
reveal.answer: Beginning Inventory + Purchases + Labor (direct manufacturing) + Materials and Supplies + Other costs = Cost of Goods Available - Ending Inventory = COGS

knowledgeCheck.question: A self-employed consultant completed a project for a client on December 28th. The client mailed a check on December 30th, which the consultant received and deposited on January 2nd of the following year. In which year is the consultant required to report the income?
options: The year the check was mailed (December 30th).
The year the check was received (January 2nd).
The year the project was completed (December 28th).
The year the payment was earned, regardless of when received (December 28th).
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under the constructive receipt doctrine, income is taxable when it is made available to you without substantial limitations or restrictions. Since the check was mailed on December 30th, the income is constructively received in that year, even if physically received in January. Note: because the taxpayer is on the cash method, the year the payment was earned does not matter.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider when the income became available to the consultant.

title: Sale of Residence
description: Master Section 121 exclusion and proper reporting of home sale gains

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Section 121 exclusion is one of the best tax breaks available! Qualified homeowners can exclude up to $500,000 of gain on their primary residence sale. This is heavily tested on the SEE.

sections.title: Section 121 Exclusion Amounts
rows: Both own, both use, neither excluded gain in 2 years

sections.title: Ownership and Use Tests
sections.content:
**Both tests must be met during the 5 years before sale:**

**Ownership Test:**
• Owned the home for at least 2 years
• OR periods that aggregate to 2 years

**Use Test:**
• Used as principal residence for at least 2 years
• OR periods that aggregate to 2 years
• Can be different 2-year periods than ownership

**The "2-out-of-5-year" rule:**
• Look back 5 years from date of sale
• Need 24 months (730 days) of ownership
• Need 24 months (730 days) of use as principal residence

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Section 121 Tests
sections.content:
**"2/2/5"** Rule:

• **2** years of ownership
• **2** years of use
• During the **5** years before sale

**Plus:** Cannot have used exclusion in prior 2 years!

sections.title: Reduced Exclusion
sections.content:
**Partial exclusion available if tests not fully met due to:**

• Change in place of employment (at least 50 miles)
• Health reasons (doctor's order)
• Unforeseen circumstances (divorce, disaster, etc.)

**Calculate reduced exclusion:**

Prorated amount = Maximum exclusion × (Short period / 2 years)

**Example:**
Owned/used 1 year due to job transfer
Reduced exclusion = $250,000 × (1/2) = $125,000

sections.title: Special Situations
rows: Surviving spouse can use $500,000 if sold within 2 years of death
Receiving spouse includes transferring spouse's ownership time
Can suspend 5-year period for up to 10 years
Must determine which is principal residence based on facts

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Depreciation Recapture
sections.content:
**If home was ever used for business or rental:**

• Section 121 exclusion does NOT apply to depreciation
• Must recapture depreciation as gain at 25% rate
• This is "unrecaptured Section 1250 gain"

**Example:**
• Total gain: $300,000
• Depreciation taken: $20,000
• Excludable: $280,000 (under limit)
• Taxable: $20,000 at 25% = $5,000 tax

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Single taxpayers exclude up to $250,000 of gain
Married filing jointly can exclude up to $500,000
Must meet 2-year ownership AND 2-year use tests in prior 5 years
Cannot have used exclusion in prior 2 years
Reduced exclusion available for job, health, unforeseen circumstances
Depreciation taken cannot be excluded - recaptured at 25%
Military can suspend the 5-year period up to 10 years

sections.title: Ownership and Use Test Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah sold her principal residence on January 1, 2024, for a significant gain. She had owned the home since January 1, 2020, but lived in it as her principal residence only from January 1, 2021, to December 31, 2022. Assuming she meets all other requirements, what is the maximum exclusion amount she can claim on the sale, if she is single?
options: She is not eligible for any exclusion.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sarah meets the ownership test because she owned the home for more than 2 years within the 5-year period before the sale. She also meets the use test because she used it as her principal residence for at least 2 years within that same period. As a single individual, she can exclude up to $250,000 of the gain.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider both the ownership and use tests, and remember the exclusion amount for single filers.

reveal.question: List three circumstances that may allow for a reduced exclusion of gain on the sale of a principal residence, even if the ownership and use tests are not fully met.
reveal.answer:
1. Change in place of employment (at least 50 miles)
2. Health reasons (doctor's order)
3. Unforeseen circumstances (e.g., divorce, disaster).

knowledgeCheck.question: John and Mary, a married couple filing jointly, sold their home due to John's new job requiring a move over 50 miles away. They owned and lived in the home for 18 months (1.5 years). Assuming they meet all other requirements, what is the maximum exclusion they can claim?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The maximum exclusion for a married couple filing jointly is $500,000. Since they qualify for a reduced exclusion due to a change in employment, the calculation is: $500,000 * (1.5 years / 2 years) = $375,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the maximum exclusion for married filing jointly and the formula for the prorated amount.

title: Retirement Income and Distributions
description: Understand taxation of IRA, 401(k), pension, and Social Security distributions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Retirement distributions are a major source of income for many clients. Knowing when distributions are taxable, how to handle early withdrawals, and the complex Social Security rules is essential.

sections.title: Traditional IRA/401(k) Distributions
sections.content:
**General Rule:**
Distributions from traditional retirement accounts are FULLY taxable as ordinary income.

**Exception for basis:**
If nondeductible contributions were made, portion of distribution is tax-free (pro-rata rule).

**Early Distribution Penalty (10%):**
Applies to distributions before age 59½ unless exception applies.

**Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs):**
• Must begin at age 73 (under SECURE 2.0)
• Penalty for failure: 25% of amount not withdrawn (was 50%)
• Reduced to 10% if corrected timely

sections.title: Roth IRA Rules
sections.content:
**Qualified Distributions (Tax-Free):**
Distribution is qualified if:
• 5-year holding period met, AND
• Age 59½, death, disability, or first-time homebuyer ($10K)

**5-Year Holding Period:**
• Starts January 1 of year first Roth created/converted
• Each conversion has its own 5-year period for under-59½

**Ordering Rules for Nonqualified:**
1. Regular contributions (always tax and penalty-free)
2. Conversions (taxable if under 5 years and under 59½)
3. Earnings (taxable and 10% penalty if nonqualified)

sections.title: Social Security Taxation
sections.content:
**Modified AGI Threshold Test:**

Provisional Income = AGI + 50% of SS benefits + tax-exempt interest

**Single/HOH Thresholds:**
• Below $25,000: 0% taxable
• $25,000 - $34,000: Up to 50% taxable
• Above $34,000: Up to 85% taxable

**MFJ Thresholds:**
• Below $32,000: 0% taxable
• $32,000 - $44,000: Up to 50% taxable
• Above $44,000: Up to 85% taxable

**Maximum 85% of benefits can ever be taxable!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: SS Calculation
sections.content:
**The Social Security calculation appears complex but follow these steps:**

1. Calculate provisional income (AGI + ½ SS + tax-exempt interest)
2. Compare to thresholds
3. Calculate using the lesser of formulas

**Key:** Tax-exempt interest IS included in provisional income!

**MFS living with spouse:** 85% is always taxable (no threshold benefit)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Traditional IRA/401(k) distributions are fully taxable (except basis)
10% early withdrawal penalty applies before age 59½ with exceptions
RMDs begin at age 73 under SECURE 2.0
Roth qualified distributions require 5-year hold plus age 59½
Social Security: up to 85% can be taxable based on provisional income
First-time homebuyer exception: IRA only, not 401(k)
Age 55 separation from service: 401(k) only, not IRA

knowledgeCheck.question: John, age 62, takes a $20,000 distribution from his Traditional IRA. He made $10,000 of nondeductible contributions over the years. What is the taxable amount of the distribution?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The taxable amount is calculated as follows: (Total Nondeductible Contributions / Total IRA Balance) * Distribution Amount = Tax-Free Portion. In this case, we assume the total IRA balance is at least $20,000. ($10,000 / $20,000) * $20,000 = $10,000 (Tax-Free). Therefore, $20,000 (Distribution) - $5,000 (Tax-Free) = $15,000 (Taxable).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the pro-rata rule when nondeductible contributions are involved.

reveal.question: When does the 5-year holding period for Roth IRA qualified distributions begin?
reveal.answer: The 5-year holding period begins on January 1st of the year the Roth IRA was first established or the year a conversion to a Roth IRA was made. Each conversion has its own separate 5-year holding period.

knowledgeCheck.question: Mary is single and has an AGI of $28,000, Social Security benefits of $10,000, and tax-exempt interest of $2,000. What percentage of her Social Security benefits could potentially be taxable?
options: It cannot be determined from the information given.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate Mary's provisional income: $28,000 (AGI) + ($10,000 * 0.50) (50% of SS) + $2,000 (Tax-Exempt Interest) = $33,000. Since her provisional income is between $25,000 and $34,000, up to 50% of her Social Security benefits could be taxable.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula for Provisional Income and the single thresholds.

sections.title: Traditional vs. Roth IRA Early Distribution Penalties

rows.label: General Rule (Before 59 1/2)
values: 10% penalty on taxable portion of distribution, plus ordinary income tax.

Order of distributions: Contributions (always tax-free and penalty-free), then conversions (taxable if within 5-year conversion period, but penalty-free), then earnings (taxable and subject to 10% penalty unless an exception applies).

rows.label: Exceptions to 10% Penalty
values: Death, disability, medical expenses exceeding 7.5% AGI, qualified higher education expenses, first-time homebuyer (up to $10,000), certain distributions to beneficiaries, etc.
Death, disability, first-time homebuyer (up to $10,000), etc. Note: Contributions can always be withdrawn tax-free and penalty-free.

title: Standard Deduction
description: Master the standard deduction amounts, additional amounts, and limitations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The TCJA nearly doubled standard deductions, making them the choice for most taxpayers. Know these amounts cold - they're on nearly every return you prepare and frequently tested on the SEE!

sections.title: Additional Standard Deduction
sections.content:
**Who Qualifies:**

**Age 65 or Older:**
• Must be 65 by January 1 of the FOLLOWING year
• Example: For 2024, must be 65 by January 1, 2025
• If born on January 1, 1960, you ARE 65 for 2024

**Blind:**
• Cannot see better than 20/200 in better eye with correction, OR
• Field of vision is 20 degrees or less

**Both 65+ AND Blind:**
• Get TWO additional amounts!
• Single taxpayer 65+ and blind: $14,600 + $3,900 = $18,500

sections.title: Dependent's Limited Standard Deduction
sections.content:
**If you CAN be claimed as someone else's dependent:**

Your standard deduction is LIMITED to the GREATER of:

1. **$1,300** (minimum), OR
2. **Earned income + $450** (up to the regular standard deduction)

**Example:**
Dependent child earns $5,000 in wages:
• Standard deduction = $5,000 + $450 = $5,450

Dependent child earns $0:
• Standard deduction = $1,300 (minimum)

Dependent child earns $15,000:
• Standard deduction = $14,600 (capped at regular amount)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Dependent Standard Deduction
sections.content:
**"Greater of $1,300 or Earned + $450"**

Remember: The FLOOR is $1,300, and the CEILING is the regular standard deduction for their filing status.

This prevents dependents with only investment income from getting the full standard deduction.

sections.title: Who CANNOT Use Standard Deduction?
sections.content:
**Must Itemize If:**

1. **MFS and spouse itemizes** - both must use same method
2. **Nonresident alien** (generally)
3. **Dual-status alien** (for dual-status year)
4. **Short tax year** due to change in accounting period
5. **Estate or trust** (Form 1041 filers)

**Note:** Married couples MUST coordinate - if one itemizes, both must itemize!

sections.title: ⚠️ TCJA Sunset Alert
sections.content:
**Standard deductions were nearly doubled by TCJA (2018-2025)**

**After 2025 (unless extended):**
• Standard deductions may revert to pre-TCJA levels (roughly half)
• More taxpayers may benefit from itemizing again
• Personal exemptions may return

**For 2024 exam:** Use current TCJA amounts, but know change is coming!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Additional amounts for 65+ and/or blind
Age 65 = 65 by January 1 of FOLLOWING year
Dependent's deduction: Greater of $1,300 or earned income + $450
MFS couples must BOTH use same method
TCJA standard deductions expire after 2025

knowledgeCheck.question: For the 2024 tax year, which of the following individuals is eligible for the additional standard deduction for age?
options: An individual born on January 2, 1960.
An individual born on December 31, 1959.
An individual who will turn 65 on January 1, 2026.
An individual who turned 64 in 2024 and will turn 65 in 2025.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The individual must be 65 by January 1 of the FOLLOWING year. An individual born on December 31, 1959, turned 65 during 2024 and therefore meets the age requirement for the 2024 tax year.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the 'January 1 of the FOLLOWING year' rule.

knowledgeCheck.question: A 17-year-old is claimed as a dependent on their parents' tax return. They earned $3,000 in wages during the tax year. What is the dependent's standard deduction?
options: The regular standard deduction amount for a single individual
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The dependent's standard deduction is limited to the GREATER of $1,300 OR earned income + $450. In this case, $3,000 (earned income) + $450 = $3,450. Since $3,450 is greater than $1,300, the standard deduction is $3,450.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate both options and choose the GREATER of the two.

reveal.question: What is the minimum standard deduction amount for an individual who CAN be claimed as a dependent on someone else's return?
reveal.answer: $1,300.  Even if their earned income plus $450 is less than $1,300, their standard deduction is $1,300.

title: Medical and Dental Expenses
description: Understand what medical expenses are deductible and how to calculate the deduction

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Medical expenses can be substantial, but most taxpayers can't deduct them due to the 7.5% AGI floor. Know exactly what qualifies and how to maximize this deduction for clients with high medical costs or lower incomes.

sections.title: The 7.5% AGI Floor
sections.content:
**Only medical expenses EXCEEDING 7.5% of AGI are deductible!**

**Calculation:**
Total qualified medical expenses
- (AGI × 7.5%)
= Deductible medical expenses

**Example:**
• AGI: $80,000
• Medical expenses: $8,000
• Floor: $80,000 × 7.5% = $6,000
• Deductible: $8,000 - $6,000 = **$2,000**

**Reality:** Combined with high standard deduction, few taxpayers benefit.

sections.title: Whose Expenses Qualify?
sections.content:
**You can deduct medical expenses paid for:**

• Yourself
• Spouse
• Dependents (at time of service OR payment)
• Child of divorced parents (either parent can deduct)
• Person who WOULD be dependent except for income or joint return test

**The person doesn't have to live with you** if they would otherwise qualify as your dependent.

sections.title: Qualified Medical Expenses
rows: Medicare Part A (if covered by SS)

sections.title: Medical Transportation
sections.content:
**Deductible transportation to/from medical care:**

**Standard Mileage Rate (2024):** 21 cents per mile
• Plus parking fees and tolls

**OR Actual Costs:**
• Gas, oil, parking, tolls
• Not: depreciation, insurance, general repairs

**Other Transportation:**
• Bus, taxi, Uber fares
• Ambulance costs
• Travel costs if medical care primary reason

**Lodging:** Up to $50 per night per person (no lavish accommodations)

sections.title: Insurance Premium Rules
sections.content:
**Deductible Premiums:**
• Health insurance (if not paid pre-tax at work)
• Medicare Part B and Part D
• Medicare Supplement (Medigap)
• Long-term care insurance (age-based limits)

**NOT Deductible:**
• Premiums paid with pre-tax dollars (employer plan)
• Medicare Part A (generally paid through SS taxes)
• Life insurance
• Disability insurance

**Self-employed:** Health insurance premiums are above-the-line deduction instead!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Timing of Payment
sections.content:
**Medical expenses are deductible in the year PAID, not incurred!**

• December 2024 bill paid January 2025 = 2025 deduction
• Credit card: Deductible when charged (not when card is paid)
• Prepaid expenses: Generally NOT deductible until service rendered

**Exception:** Expenses paid from HSA/FSA are not also deductible

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Only expenses exceeding 7.5% of AGI are deductible
Can deduct expenses for yourself, spouse, and dependents
Prescription drugs deductible; OTC generally not
Medical mileage: 21¢/mile (2024)
Insurance premiums deductible if paid after-tax
Deductible in year PAID, not year of service
Self-employed health insurance is above-the-line, not here

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer Jones has an Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) of $60,000. During the year, Jones incurred $5,000 in qualified medical expenses. How much can Jones deduct as a medical expense?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The deductible amount is calculated as follows: $5,000 (Medical Expenses) - ($60,000 (AGI) * 0.075 (7.5% AGI Threshold)) = $5,000 - $4,500 = $500. Therefore, the correct answer is $500.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to apply the 7.5% AGI threshold before determining the deductible amount.

reveal.question: List the categories of individuals whose medical expenses you can potentially deduct.
reveal.answer: You, your spouse, your dependents (at time of service OR payment), a child of divorced parents (either parent can deduct), and a person who WOULD be your dependent except for the gross income test or joint return test.

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer Smith drove 200 miles round trip to a specialist for treatment. Smith paid $10 in tolls and $5 for parking. Using the standard mileage rate for 2024, what amount can Smith include as a medical expense for transportation?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The standard mileage rate for medical transportation in 2024 is 21 cents per mile. 200 miles * $0.21 = $42. Add the tolls and parking fees: $42 + $10 + $5 = $57. Therefore, the correct answer is $57.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to include parking fees and tolls in addition to the mileage rate.

title: State and Local Taxes (SALT)
description: Master the SALT deduction rules including the $10,000 cap

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The TCJA's $10,000 SALT cap was one of the most controversial changes. This limit significantly affects taxpayers in high-tax states. Understanding what counts toward the cap and planning strategies is essential.

sections.title: The $10,000 SALT Cap
sections.content:
**TCJA Limitation (2018-2025):**

Total of ALL state and local taxes limited to:
• **$10,000** for all filers (Single, HOH, MFJ)
• **$5,000** if Married Filing Separately

**This includes:**
• State/local income taxes (OR sales taxes if elected)
• Real property taxes
• Personal property taxes

**Foreign taxes do NOT count** toward the $10,000 limit.

sections.title: What Taxes Are Deductible?
rows: Deduct on Schedule C, not here

sections.title: Income Tax vs. Sales Tax Election
sections.content:
**You can deduct EITHER:**

**State and Local Income Taxes:** Actual amounts paid (withholding + estimates + prior year payments)

**OR**

**State and Local Sales Taxes:**
• Actual receipts (must keep all receipts), OR
• IRS tables (based on income and state)
• Plus actual tax on major purchases (cars, boats, etc.)

**Can't deduct both!**

**When to choose sales tax:** States with no income tax (TX, FL, WA, NV, etc.)

sections.title: Real Property Tax Details
sections.content:
**Deductible real estate taxes:**
• Taxes on your home
• Taxes on vacation homes
• Taxes on land you own

**NOT Deductible:**
• Special assessments for improvements (sidewalks, sewers)
• Trash collection fees
• Homeowner association dues
• Transfer taxes

**Rental property taxes:** Deducted on Schedule E, NOT here!

sections.title: Personal Property Tax
sections.content:
**Deductible if:**
• Charged by state or local government
• Based on VALUE of the property (ad valorem)
• Imposed on personal property (cars, boats)

**Example: Vehicle Registration**
Many states combine a:
• Flat fee (NOT deductible)
• Value-based tax (deductible)

**Only the value-based portion qualifies!**

Registration statements often specify the deductible amount.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: SALT Workarounds
sections.content:
**Some states enacted "SALT cap workarounds":**

Pass-through entity (PTE) taxes allow business owners to:
• Pay state tax at entity level
• Deduct as business expense (no $10K cap)
• Get credit on individual return

**IRS Notice 2020-75:** Approved PTE taxes as valid workaround.

**This is state-specific - not every state has this option!**

sections.title: Planning Considerations
sections.content:
**Strategies under the SALT cap:**

• Prepaying next year's taxes may not help (if already over $10K)
• Consider state-specific PTE elections for business owners
• Charitable contributions as alternative (SALT alternatives)
• Business-related taxes are NOT subject to cap

**SALT cap expires after 2025** (unless extended) - may change planning!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SALT deduction capped at $10,000 ($5,000 MFS)
Includes state/local income OR sales taxes (not both)
Real property and personal property taxes included in cap
Foreign taxes NOT subject to the $10,000 cap
Personal property tax must be value-based to deduct
Business taxes deducted separately, not subject to cap
TCJA SALT cap set to expire after 2025

knowledgeCheck.question: Harold and Maude are married filing jointly. They paid $6,000 in state income taxes, $5,000 in real property taxes, and $1,000 in personal property taxes during the tax year. What is the total amount of state and local taxes they can deduct on Schedule A?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The total of their state and local taxes is $12,000 ($6,000 + $5,000 + $1,000). However, the SALT deduction is limited to $10,000 for married filing jointly.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the SALT limit for married filing jointly.

reveal.question: Besides using IRS tables, how else can you calculate the state and local sales tax deduction?
reveal.answer: You can deduct the actual amount of state and local sales taxes paid, but you must keep all receipts to substantiate the deduction. You can also use the IRS tables and add any sales tax paid on major purchases (e.g., a car).

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT deductible as a real property tax on Schedule A?
options: Taxes paid on your primary residence
Taxes paid on a vacation home
Special assessment for a new sidewalk benefiting your property
Taxes paid on vacant land held for investment
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Special assessments for improvements, such as sidewalks or sewers, are generally not deductible as real property taxes. They are added to the basis of the property.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider whether the expense is a general tax or a payment for a specific benefit.

title: Home Mortgage Interest
description: Master the rules for deducting mortgage interest including debt limitations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Home mortgage interest is one of the most valuable itemized deductions, but TCJA changed the rules significantly. Understanding acquisition debt limits, what qualifies, and how refinancing works is essential for homeowner clients.

sections.title: TCJA Changes: Two Sets of Rules
Qualified Residence Interest
sections.content:
**Interest deductible on:**

**Acquisition Indebtedness:**
• Debt to buy, build, or substantially improve a qualified home
• Secured by the residence
• Limited to $750,000 ($375,000 MFS) for post-12/14/2017 debt
• Grandfathered $1,000,000 limit for pre-12/15/2017 debt

**Qualified Home:**
• Main residence, AND
• One other residence (vacation home)
• Can be a house, condo, co-op, mobile home, or boat with sleeping, cooking, and toilet facilities

sections.title: Home Equity Interest Under TCJA
sections.content:
**2018-2025 Rules:**

Home equity loan interest is:
• **Deductible** ONLY if used to buy, build, or substantially improve the home securing the debt
• **NOT Deductible** if used for other purposes (car, vacation, debt consolidation)

**Example:**
• $50,000 home equity loan to add a new room: **DEDUCTIBLE**
• $50,000 home equity loan to pay off credit cards: **NOT DEDUCTIBLE**

**The key is the USE of proceeds, not the name of the loan!**

sections.title: Mixed-Use Debt
sections.content:
**If debt is used for multiple purposes:**

• Must allocate between acquisition and personal use
• Only acquisition portion interest is deductible

**Example:**
$400,000 cash-out refinance:
• $300,000 to pay off old mortgage (acquisition)
• $100,000 used for other purposes (personal)

**Result:** Only 75% of interest is deductible (300,000/400,000)

sections.title: Refinancing Rules
sections.content:
**General Rule:**
Refinanced debt treated as acquisition debt to extent of old debt's acquisition balance.

**Cash-out refinancing:**
• Only acquisition portion retains acquisition status
• New (cash-out) portion must be used for home improvements to be deductible

**Points on Refinancing:**
• Generally must be amortized over life of loan
• Exception: Points for home improvement portion may be currently deductible

sections.title: Points Deduction
sections.content:
**Points paid to obtain a mortgage:**

**Fully Deductible in Year Paid If:**
• Loan secured by main home
• Points are established business practice in the area
• Points don't exceed amount generally charged
• Computed as a percentage of principal
• Shown on settlement statement
• For purchase or improvement of main home
• Cash method taxpayer
• Funds provided at closing cover points

**Otherwise:** Amortize over life of loan

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: MFS Limit
sections.content:
**Married Filing Separately:**

• Acquisition debt limit is HALF: $375,000
• This applies even if only one spouse has mortgage!

**Impact:** MFS couples with large mortgages may lose deductions

**Planning:** Consider if MFJ might be better even with other MFS benefits

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Only acquisition debt interest is deductible under TCJA
Home equity interest: Deductible ONLY if used to improve the home
Up to 2 qualified homes (main residence + one other)
Points on purchase: Usually fully deductible; refinance: amortize
MFS reduces limit to $375,000
TCJA rules expire after 2025 unless extended

knowledgeCheck.question: In 2024, Sarah refinanced her home mortgage for $800,000. The original mortgage was taken out in 2016. How much home mortgage interest can she deduct, assuming she itemizes and meets all other requirements for deductibility?
options: Interest on the full $800,000
Interest on $750,000
Interest on $1,000,000
Interest on the amount of the original mortgage balance prior to the refinance.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The grandfathered $1,000,000 limit applies to debt incurred before December 15, 2017. However, a refinance cannot exceed the original amount of the debt. Therefore, the deductible interest is limited to the amount of the original mortgage balance.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the date the original debt was incurred and the rules regarding refinancing.

reveal.question: Under the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) from 2018-2025, when is interest on a home equity loan deductible?
reveal.answer: Interest on a home equity loan is only deductible if the loan proceeds are used to buy, build, or substantially improve the home that secures the loan. If used for any other purpose (e.g., car, vacation), the interest is not deductible.

knowledgeCheck.question: John took out a $500,000 cash-out refinance on his home. He used $400,000 to pay off his existing mortgage and $100,000 to pay off credit card debt. Assuming he itemizes and meets all other requirements, on what amount of the $500,000 can he deduct home mortgage interest?
options: Interest on the full $500,000
Interest on $400,000
Interest on $100,000
No interest is deductible because it was a cash-out refinance.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Because $400,000 of the refinance was used to pay off the original mortgage (acquisition debt), the interest on that portion is deductible. The $100,000 used for credit card debt is considered personal use, and the interest on that portion is not deductible.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to allocate the debt between acquisition and personal use.

title: Standard vs. Itemized Deductions
description: Determine when to itemize and understand the standard deduction amounts

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Every individual return requires a deduction choice. The TCJA nearly doubled standard deductions, so many taxpayers no longer benefit from itemizing. Know both options to advise clients correctly!

sections.title: Who CANNOT Use Standard Deduction?
sections.content:
**Must itemize if:**

• MFS and spouse itemizes (both must use same method)
• Nonresident alien or dual-status alien
• Estate or trust
• Short tax year due to accounting period change

**Can be claimed as dependent:**
• Standard deduction limited to GREATER of:
  - $1,300, or
  - Earned income + $450 (up to normal amount)

sections.title: Itemized Deduction Categories
content: Medical and dental expenses (exceeding 7.5% AGI floor)
Taxes paid (SALT limited to $10,000)
Home mortgage interest (limited to $750,000 debt)
Charitable contributions (various AGI limits)
Casualty losses (federally declared disasters only)
Other itemized deductions (limited under TCJA)
items: **Medical**: Expenses > 7.5% of AGI.
**Taxes (SALT)**: State/Local Income or Sales Tax + Property Tax (Capped at $10k).
**Interest**: Mortgage interest (on up to $750k debt) & Investment interest.
**Charity**: Contributions to qualified organizations (Cash: 60% AGI limit).
**Casualty**: Federally declared disaster losses only.
**Other**: Gambling losses to extent of winnings.

sections.title: ⚠️ TCJA Changes (Expires 2026)
sections.content:
**Current law through 2025:**

• Standard deduction nearly doubled
• SALT deduction capped at $10,000
• Miscellaneous itemized deductions suspended
• Home equity loan interest not deductible (unless home acquisition)
• Casualty losses only for federal disasters

**2026 sunset:** These revert to pre-TCJA rules unless extended!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Standard deduction is $14,600 single / $29,200 MFJ (2024)
Additional deduction for age 65+ and/or blind
MFS must both use same method (standard or itemized)
Dependents have limited standard deduction
SALT cap ($10,000) applies through 2025
Compare itemized total to standard deduction - use larger

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals MUST itemize deductions on their federal income tax return, regardless of whether their itemized deductions exceed the standard deduction?
options: A single taxpayer with $5,000 in itemized deductions.
A married taxpayer filing separately whose spouse itemizes deductions.
A qualifying widow(er) with dependent children.
A U.S. citizen residing abroad with substantial foreign income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Married taxpayers filing separately must both use the same method of deduction. If one spouse itemizes, the other spouse must also itemize, even if their itemized deductions are less than the standard deduction.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the filing status requirements for itemizing deductions.

reveal.question: How is the standard deduction calculated for an individual who can be claimed as a dependent on another person's return?
reveal.answer: The standard deduction for an individual who can be claimed as a dependent is limited to the GREATER of $1,300 or earned income plus $45. This amount cannot exceed the regular standard deduction for their filing status.

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah is a college student who is claimed as a dependent on her parents' tax return. In 2024, she earned $2,500 from a part-time job. What is the amount of Sarah's standard deduction?
options: $13,850 (Single Standard Deduction)
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sarah's standard deduction is the GREATER of $1,300 or her earned income plus $45. Her earned income plus $45 is $2,500 + $45 = $2,545. Since $2,545 is greater than $1,300, her standard deduction is $2,545.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: Greater of $1,300 or Earned Income + $45

title: Medical and Dental Expenses
description: Learn what medical expenses are deductible and how to calculate the deduction

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Medical expenses can be substantial, but few taxpayers can deduct them due to the 7.5% AGI floor. Know what qualifies and how to maximize this deduction for clients with high medical costs.

sections.title: The 7.5% AGI Floor
sections.content:
**Only expenses EXCEEDING 7.5% of AGI are deductible!**

**Example:**
• AGI: $100,000
• Medical expenses: $10,000
• Floor: $100,000 × 7.5% = $7,500
• Deductible amount: $10,000 - $7,500 = **$2,500**

With high standard deductions, many taxpayers get no benefit even with significant medical costs.

sections.title: Qualified Medical Expenses
rows: Weight loss programs for general health
Medicare Part A (if covered by SS)

sections.title: Whose Expenses Qualify?
sections.content:
**You can deduct medical expenses for:**

• Yourself
• Your spouse
• Your dependents (using dependency rules)
• Person who WOULD be your dependent except for:
  - Filing a joint return
  - Having gross income ≥ $5,050
  - You being claimed as dependent

**Key:** The gross income limit doesn't block medical expense deduction!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Only expenses exceeding 7.5% of AGI are deductible
Cosmetic procedures not deductible unless for injury/deformity
Prescriptions and insulin are deductible
Can deduct for dependents and "almost-dependents"
Medical insurance premiums deductible if not pre-tax
Must itemize to claim medical expense deduction

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer Smith has an Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) of $60,000. During the tax year, Smith incurred $6,000 in qualified medical expenses. What amount, if any, can Smith deduct as medical expenses on Schedule A?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The deductible amount is calculated as follows: AGI ($60,000) * 7.5% = $4,500 (AGI threshold). Medical expenses ($6,000) - AGI threshold ($4,500) = $1,500. Therefore, Smith can deduct $1,500.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to apply the 7.5% AGI floor before determining the deductible amount.

sections.title: Whose Medical Expenses Can You Deduct?

reveal.question: List four categories of individuals whose medical expenses you may be able to deduct on your tax return.
reveal.answer: 1. Yourself. 2. Your spouse. 3. Your dependents (meeting dependency rules). 4. An individual who would be your dependent except for: (a) filing a joint return, (b) having gross income of $5,050 or more, or (c) being claimed as a dependent on another person's return.

knowledgeCheck.question: John provides over half the support for his mother, Mary. Mary's gross income is $6,000, and she does not file a joint return. John cannot claim Mary as a dependent because her gross income exceeds the limit. Can John deduct the medical expenses he pays for Mary?
options: No, because Mary's gross income exceeds the dependency limit.
Yes, because John provides over half of Mary's support and she would be his dependent except for the gross income test.
Yes, but only if Mary is considered disabled.
No, because Mary is not related to John.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: John can deduct Mary's medical expenses because she would be his dependent except for exceeding the gross income limit. The gross income limitation does not prevent the deduction of medical expenses in this scenario.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the exception to the gross income test when deducting medical expenses for someone who would otherwise be your dependent.

title: State and Local Taxes (SALT)
description: Understand the $10,000 SALT deduction cap and what taxes qualify

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The SALT cap is one of the most significant TCJA changes affecting high-tax state residents. The $10,000 limit ($5,000 MFS) drastically changed who benefits from itemizing.

sections.title: SALT Deduction Components
sections.content:
**State and local income taxes** OR **general sales taxes** (choose one)
**PLUS:**
**Real property taxes** (primary residence 
+ vacation home)
**Personal property taxes** (if based on value)

**ALL COMBINED LIMITED TO $10,000 ($5,000 MFS)**

sections.title: Income Tax vs. Sales Tax Election
sections.content:
**Choose the larger of:**

**State/Local Income Taxes:**
• Actual withholding and estimated payments
• Prior year state tax paid (if claimed when paid)

**General Sales Taxes:**
• Actual receipts (must keep records), OR
• IRS tables (based on income, family size, location)
• Add actual sales tax on major purchases

**Tip:** Sales tax election often better in states with no income tax (TX, FL, WA, etc.)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Foreign Taxes
sections.content:
**Foreign income taxes are NOT part of SALT!**

• Foreign taxes deducted separately (OR claimed as credit)
• Not subject to $10,000 cap
• Most taxpayers benefit more from foreign tax credit

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SALT cap is $10,000 ($5,000 MFS) through 2025
Choose income taxes OR sales taxes (not both)
Property taxes on main home and vacation home qualify
Foreign taxes not subject to SALT cap
Cap scheduled to expire after 2025

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer Sarah is single and itemizing deductions. In 2023, she paid $6,000 in state income taxes, $3,000 in real property taxes on her primary residence, and $2,000 in personal property taxes (based on value) on her vehicle. What is the amount of Sarah's deductible SALT?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sarah's total SALT is $6,000 (state income tax) + $3,000 (real property tax) + $2,000 (personal property tax) = $11,000. However, the SALT deduction is limited to $10,000. Therefore, Sarah can deduct $10,000. However, we must consider the instruction to select the larger of income tax or sales tax. Since income tax is larger, we select that. Thus, 6000+3000+2000=11000. Deduction capped at $10,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the SALT deduction limit for single taxpayers.

reveal.question: List the components that make up the SALT deduction.
reveal.answer: The SALT deduction consists of the larger of state/local income taxes or general sales taxes, plus real property taxes (on primary and vacation homes), and personal property taxes (if based on value). The total is then subject to a $10,000 limit ($5,000 MFS).

sections.title: Income Tax vs. Sales Tax Election

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer John resides in a state with no income tax. He paid $8,000 in real property taxes on his primary residence and can document $3,000 in general sales taxes paid throughout the year. John is single. What is the maximum amount of SALT he can deduct?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since there is no state income tax, John must use the general sales tax. His deductible SALT is $3,000 (sales tax) + $8,000 (property tax) = $11,000. However, the deduction is capped at $10,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to choose the larger of state/local income taxes or sales taxes. Since the state has no income tax, the sales tax figure is used.

sections.title: Income Tax vs. Sales Tax: Key Differences in Calculation

rows.label: Calculation Methods
values: Actual withholding and estimated payments; Prior year state tax paid (if deducted when paid)
Actual receipts (requires detailed records) OR IRS tables (based on income, family size, location), plus actual sales tax on certain items (e.g., vehicles)

rows.label: Record Keeping Requirements
values: W-2 forms, estimated tax payment records
Detailed receipts if using the actual method; None needed if using IRS tables (except for certain large purchases)

rows.label: Availability
values: Available in states with income tax.
Available even in states with no income tax.

title: Mortgage Interest Deduction
description: Learn the rules for deducting home mortgage interest

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The mortgage interest deduction is a significant tax benefit for homeowners. TCJA reduced the debt limit and changed home equity rules - know the current law!

sections.title: Acquisition Debt Definition
sections.content:
**Acquisition debt is debt used to:**
• Buy the home
• Build the home
• Substantially improve the home

**Secured by the home (main or second home)**

**Home equity debt (HELOC for other purposes):**
• NOT deductible 2018-2025 unless used to improve the home
• Interest on HELOC for kitchen remodel = deductible
• Interest on HELOC to pay off credit cards = NOT deductible

sections.title: Mortgage Points
sections.content:
**Points on purchase of main home:**
• Generally fully deductible in year paid
• Must be for main residence
• Charging points must be established practice in area
• Points stated on settlement statement

**Points on refinance:**
• Must be amortized over life of loan
• Exception: Portion for home improvements may be current
• Old loan unamortized points deductible when refinanced

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Acquisition debt limit is $750,000 for debt after 12/15/2017
Home equity interest not deductible unless used to improve home
Points on purchase generally deductible immediately
Points on refinance must be amortized
Applies to main home and one second home

knowledgeCheck.question: In 2024, Sarah used a $50,000 home equity line of credit (HELOC) secured by her primary residence to purchase a new car. She also paid $2,000 in interest on the HELOC during the year. How much of the $2,000 interest expense can Sarah deduct on Schedule A of Form 1040?
options: An amount up to the first $100,000 of the HELOC
An amount determined by multiplying $2,000 by the ratio of the cost of home improvements to the total HELOC amount.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Interest paid on home equity debt is not deductible unless the debt is used to buy, build, or substantially improve the taxpayer's home. Since the HELOC was used to purchase a car, the interest is not deductible.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the restrictions on home equity debt interest deductibility from 2018-2025.

reveal.question: What is the general rule for deducting points paid on a refinance of a mortgage?
reveal.answer: Points paid on a refinance must be amortized over the life of the loan. An exception applies to the portion of points paid that relates to home improvements; this portion can be deducted immediately.

sections.title: Acquisition Debt vs. Home Equity Debt (2018-2025)

rows.label: Definition
values: Debt used to buy, build, or substantially improve a home, secured by that home.
Debt secured by a home, often a line of credit (HELOC), that can be used for various purposes.

rows.label: Interest Deductibility (2018-2025)
values: Generally deductible, subject to debt limits.
Deductible only if used to buy, build, or substantially improve the home.

rows.label: Use of Funds
values: Specifically for acquiring or improving the home.
Can be used for any purpose, but deductibility depends on the use.

title: Charitable Contributions
description: Master the rules for deducting donations to qualified charities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Charitable giving is both personally rewarding and tax-advantageous when done correctly. Know the limits, documentation rules, and special provisions for maximum benefit.

sections.title: Qualified Organizations
sections.content:
**Deductible contributions to:**
• Churches, synagogues, mosques
• Nonprofit educational organizations
• Nonprofit hospitals
• Public parks and recreation organizations
• United Way, Red Cross, Salvation Army
• Volunteer fire companies
• 501(c)(3) organizations

**NOT deductible:**
• Individuals (no matter how needy)
• Political organizations or candidates
• Social clubs
• Foreign organizations (generally)
• Contributions for which you receive equal value

sections.title: Property Donations
sections.content:
**Appreciated capital gain property held > 1 year:**
• Deduct fair market value
• 30% AGI limit applies
• Can elect 50% limit with basis-only deduction

**Ordinary income property:**
• Deduct the lesser of FMV or basis
• Includes inventory, artwork by creator

**Vehicle, boat, or airplane donations:**
• If charity sells for > $500, limited to sales price
• If used significantly by charity, FMV allowed
• Form 1098-C required for acknowledgment

sections.title: Special Rules
sections.content:
**Qualified Charitable Distribution (QCD):**
• Age 70½ or older
• Direct transfer from IRA to charity
• Up to $105,000 per year (2024)
• Satisfies RMD but not included in income
• Better than deduction for many taxpayers

**Bunching Strategy:**
• Contribute multiple years' donations in one year
• Itemize that year, standard deduction other years
• Donor-advised funds facilitate this

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Cash contributions limited to 60% of AGI
Appreciated property contributions limited to 30% of AGI
Written acknowledgment required for $250+ donations
Qualified appraisal needed for $5,000+ noncash donations
QCD is powerful tool for those 70½+ with RMDs
Carryforward available for 5 years if limits exceeded

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer A donates stock held for two years to a qualified charity. The stock has a fair market value of $50,000 and an adjusted basis of $10,000. Taxpayer A's adjusted gross income (AGI) is $100,000. Assuming no other charitable contributions, what is the maximum amount Taxpayer A can deduct this year, and what happens to any excess contribution?
options: $30,000; the remaining $20,000 can be carried forward for up to five years.
$50,000; the full amount is deductible because it's appreciated property.
$10,000; only the basis is deductible because the FMV exceeds 30% of AGI.
$50,000; the excess is not deductible.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The deduction for appreciated capital gain property is limited to 30% of AGI. In this case, 30% of $100,000 is $30,000. The remaining $20,000 ($50,000 - $30,000) can be carried forward for up to five years.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the AGI limits for appreciated property and the carryover rules.

reveal.question: True or False: A contribution to a needy individual is deductible as a charitable contribution.
reveal.answer: False. Contributions to specific individuals, no matter how needy, are generally not deductible. Deductions are only allowed for contributions to qualified organizations.

rows.label: Deduction Amount
values: Fair Market Value (FMV), subject to AGI limits
Lesser of FMV or Basis

rows.label: AGI Limit
values: 30% (or 50% with basis-only election)

rows.label: Examples
values: Stock, Real Estate
Inventory, Artwork by the creator

title: Tax Computation and Rates
description: Master the tax rate structure and computation for individuals

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Understanding how tax is computed is fundamental! Know the difference between marginal and effective rates, how ordinary and capital gains income are taxed differently, and how to use tax tables vs. rate schedules.

sections.title: Marginal vs. Effective Tax Rate
sections.content:
**Marginal Rate:**
• The rate on your LAST dollar of taxable income
• The rate that applies if you earn one more dollar
• Used for tax planning decisions

**Effective Rate (Average Rate):**
• Total tax ÷ Total taxable income
• Your overall "average" tax rate
• Usually much lower than marginal rate

**Example (Single, $100,000 taxable income):**
• Marginal rate: 22%
• Effective rate: approximately 17%

sections.title: How Tax Computation Works
sections.content:
**Step-by-Step:**

1. Calculate **Gross Income**
2. Subtract **Adjustments** (above-the-line) = **AGI**
3. Subtract **Deductions** (standard or itemized) = **Taxable Income**
4. Apply **Tax Rates** to taxable income = **Tentative Tax**
5. Add **Other Taxes** (SE tax, AMT, NIIT, etc.)
6. Subtract **Credits** = **Total Tax**
7. Subtract **Withholding/Payments** = **Amount Due/Refund**

sections.title: Tax Tables vs. Tax Rate Schedules
sections.content:
**Tax Tables:**
• Income in $50 increments
• Used for taxable income under $100,000
• IRS provides tables in instructions

**Tax Rate Schedules:**
• Used for taxable income $100,000+
• Calculate tax using the rate schedule formula
• Same result as tables but for higher incomes

**Both provide same answer** - tables are just for convenience.

sections.title: ⚠️ TCJA Rate Sunset
sections.content:
**Current rates expire after 2025!**

**If TCJA sunsets:**
• Rates revert to pre-2018 levels
• 10%, 15%, 25%, 28%, 33%, 35%, 39.6%
• Different bracket thresholds

**For 2024 exam:** Use current TCJA rates, but know they may change!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Marginal rate = rate on last dollar; effective rate = total tax / income
Long-term capital gains: 0%, 15%, or 20% based on income
Tax tables for income under $100,000; rate schedules for $100,000+
Tax computation: Gross Income → AGI → Taxable Income → Tax
TCJA rates expire after 2025 unless extended

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer with a taxable income of $50,000 falls into the 22% marginal tax bracket. After calculating their total tax liability, it is determined they paid $7,500 in income taxes. What is their effective tax rate?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The effective tax rate is calculated as total tax paid divided by taxable income. In this case, $7,500 / $50,000 = 0.15, or 15%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: Effective Tax Rate = Total Tax / Taxable Income

reveal.question: List the steps in order to compute your tax liability, starting with Gross Income.
reveal.answer:
1. Calculate Gross Income
2. Subtract Adjustments to Income (above-the-line) to arrive at AGI
3. Subtract Deductions (standard or itemized) to arrive at Taxable Income
4. Apply Tax Rates to taxable income to arrive at Tentative Tax
5. Add Other Taxes (SE tax, AMT, NIIT, etc.)
6. Subtract Credits to arrive at Total Tax Liability

rows.label: Definition
values: The tax rate applied to the last dollar of taxable income.
The average rate of tax paid on total taxable income.

rows.label: Calculation
values: Determined by the tax bracket the taxpayer falls into.
Calculated as Total Tax / Taxable Income.

rows.label: Use
values: Used for future tax planning and determining the impact of additional income or deductions.
Provides an overview of the overall tax burden.

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer is currently in the 24% marginal tax bracket. If they are able to take an additional deduction of $1,000, by how much will their tax liability decrease?
options: It depends on their effective tax rate.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The tax liability will decrease by the amount of the deduction multiplied by the marginal tax rate. In this case, $1,000 * 0.24 = $240.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the marginal tax rate when considering the impact of changes at the margin.

title: Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT)
description: Understand the parallel AMT system and who may be subject to it

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: While TCJA significantly reduced AMT exposure, it still affects some taxpayers, especially those exercising ISOs or with large SALT deductions. Understanding AMT is essential for high-income client planning.

sections.title: What is AMT?
sections.content:
**A Parallel Tax System:**

The AMT is a separate tax calculation that:
• Starts with taxable income
• Adds back certain "preference items" and adjustments
• Allows a different exemption
• Applies different (flatter) tax rates

**You pay the HIGHER of:**
• Regular tax, OR
• Tentative Minimum Tax (if higher)

**The difference is AMT liability.**

sections.title: AMT Tax Rates
sections.content:
**AMT uses only TWO rates:**

• **26%** on AMT income up to $232,600 ($116,300 MFS)
• **28%** on AMT income above that threshold

**Capital gains in AMT:**
• Still taxed at preferential rates (0%, 15%, 20%)
• But can trigger AMT due to higher AMTI

sections.title: The ISO Problem
sections.content:
**Incentive Stock Options and AMT:**

**Regular Tax:**
• No income recognized when ISO exercised
• Taxed as capital gain when stock sold

**AMT:**
• The SPREAD (FMV - exercise price) is AMT income when exercised
• Can create significant AMT liability
• Even if stock value drops before sale!

**Example:**
• Exercise price: $10/share
• FMV at exercise: $50/share
• Spread: $40/share × 1,000 shares = $40,000 AMT income

sections.title: AMT Credit
sections.content:
**If you pay AMT in one year:**

You may get a credit in future years when:
• Regular tax exceeds tentative minimum tax
• Credit reduces regular tax (not below TMT)

**The AMT credit:**
• Applies to "deferral items" (like ISOs, depreciation)
• NOT to permanent items (like SALT addback)
• Carries forward indefinitely

**Form 8801 computes the credit.**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AMT is a parallel tax system with different rules
Pay the higher of regular tax or tentative minimum tax
AMT exemption: $85,700 single / $133,300 MFJ (2024)
AMT rates: 26% and 28% (flatter than regular tax)
ISO exercise spread is a major AMT trigger
AMT credit available for deferral preference items
TCJA greatly reduced AMT exposure for most taxpayers

knowledgeCheck.question: A single taxpayer has Alternative Minimum Taxable Income (AMTI) of $250,000. What AMT rate applies to the portion of their AMTI exceeding $232,600?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The AMT uses two rates: 26% on AMTI up to $232,600 and 28% on AMTI above that amount. In this case, the portion exceeding the threshold is taxed at 28%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the two AMT rates and the income threshold.

reveal.question: What effect do preference items generally have on Alternative Minimum Taxable Income (AMTI)?
reveal.answer: Preference items generally INCREASE Alternative Minimum Taxable Income (AMTI). They are items that are added back to taxable income when calculating AMTI, potentially triggering or increasing AMT liability.

rows.label: Tax Base
values: Taxable Income
Alternative Minimum Taxable Income (AMTI)

rows.label: Tax Rates
values: Progressive (multiple brackets)
Two rates: 26% and 28%

rows.label: Capital Gains
values: Preferential Rates (0%, 15%, 20%)
Preferential Rates (0%, 15%, 20%) - impact AMTI

rows.label: Exemption
values: Standard Deduction/Itemized Deductions
AMT Exemption (phase-out applies)

title: Self-Employment Tax
description: Master the calculation of self-employment tax for sole proprietors and partners

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Self-employed individuals pay BOTH the employee AND employer portions of Social Security and Medicare! This 15.3% tax surprises many first-time entrepreneurs. Know how to calculate it and explain it to clients.

sections.title: Who Pays SE Tax?
sections.content:
**Self-employment tax applies to:**

• Sole proprietors (Schedule C income)
• General partners in partnerships
• Self-employed farmers (Schedule F)
• Members of LLCs taxed as partnerships (if active)
• Church employee income ≥ $108.28

**Does NOT apply to:**
• S corporation shareholders on wages (W-2 income)
• Limited partners (generally - distributive share only)
• Rental income (generally passive, not SE)
• Dividend and interest income

sections.title: Net Earnings Calculation
sections.content:
**Step 1: Net Earnings from Self-Employment**

Schedule C net profit (or loss)
× 92.35%
= Net earnings from self-employment

**Why 92.35%?**
This approximates the employer portion deduction that employees get. (100% - 7.65% = 92.35%)

**Minimum threshold:** SE tax only applies if net earnings ≥ $400

sections.title: SE Tax Calculation Example
sections.content:
**Schedule C net profit: $100,000**

**Step 1:** Net earnings = $100,000 × 92.35% = $92,350

**Step 2:** Social Security portion
$92,350 × 12.4% = $11,451.40

**Step 3:** Medicare portion
$92,350 × 2.9% = $2,678.15

**Step 4:** Total SE Tax
$11,451.40 + $2,678.15 = **$14,129.55**

**Step 5:** Deductible portion (above-the-line)
$14,129.55 × 50% = **$7,064.78**

sections.title: The 50% Deduction
sections.content:
**To equalize with employees:**

Employees don't pay tax on employer's share of FICA.
Self-employed get to DEDUCT half of SE tax.

**This is an ABOVE-THE-LINE deduction!**
• Reduces AGI
• Available even if taking standard deduction
• Reported on Schedule 1, Line 15

**Does NOT reduce net earnings for SE tax calculation** - calculated after SE tax is computed.

sections.title: Combined with Wages
sections.content:
**If you also have W-2 wages:**

The Social Security wage base ($168,600) is SHARED:
• W-2 wages count first
• Only remaining room counts for SE income

**Example:**
• W-2 wages: $150,000
• Schedule C profit: $50,000
• SE net earnings: $50,000 × 92.35% = $46,175
• SS wage room: $168,600 - $150,000 = $18,600
• SS portion of SE: $18,600 × 12.4% = $2,306.40
• Medicare (all): $46,175 × 2.9% = $1,339.08

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SE tax rate: 15.3% (12.4% SS + 2.9% Medicare)
Applies to net earnings × 92.35%
Social Security portion caps at $168,600 (2024)
SE tax only applies if net earnings ≥ $400
50% of SE tax is deductible above-the-line
W-2 wages reduce remaining SS wage base for SE income
S corp owners pay FICA only on wages, not distributions

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals is subject to self-employment tax?
options: A shareholder in an S corporation receiving a salary of $75,000.
A limited partner in a real estate partnership who does not materially participate in the partnership's operations.
A sole proprietor with a Schedule C net profit of $500.
A church employee who receives $95 in income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Self-employment tax applies to sole proprietors with net earnings of $400 or more. The S corporation shareholder pays employment taxes (FICA) on their salary. Limited partners are generally not subject to self-employment tax unless they are actively involved in the business. Church employee income is subject to SE tax only if it is $108.28 or more.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the minimum threshold for self-employment income and the nature of the income.

reveal.question: Explain why self-employment income is multiplied by 92.35% when calculating self-employment tax.
reveal.answer: The 92.35% factor approximates the deduction for one-half of self-employment tax, which is equivalent to the employer's portion of FICA taxes. This allows self-employed individuals to deduct the equivalent of the employer's share of social security and Medicare taxes, similar to how employers deduct their portion of these taxes for their employees.

knowledgeCheck.question: John reported $10,000 net profit on his Schedule C. What is John's net earnings from self-employment?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: To calculate net earnings from self-employment, multiply the Schedule C net profit by 92.35%. In this case, $10,000 * 0.9235 = $9,235.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to apply the 92.35% factor to the Schedule C net profit.

title: Net Investment Income Tax
description: Understand the 3.8% NIIT and when it applies

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The 3.8% Net Investment Income Tax catches high earners who might otherwise pay little or no Medicare tax on investment income. Know the thresholds and what income is included!

sections.title: NIIT Basics
sections.content:
**The Tax:**
• 3.8% surtax on investment income
• Applies to individuals, estates, and trusts
• Created by the Affordable Care Act (2013)

**The Formula:**
3.8% × LESSER of:
• Net investment income, OR
• MAGI exceeding threshold

sections.title: What is Net Investment Income?
sections.content:
**INCLUDES:**
• Interest
• Dividends (qualified and ordinary)
• Capital gains (including from home sale if over exclusion)
• Rental and royalty income (if passive)
• Passive business income
• Annuity distributions (taxable portion)

**EXCLUDES:**
• Wages and self-employment income
• Active business income
• Distributions from qualified retirement plans
• Tax-exempt bond interest
• Self-employment income (already subject to SE tax)

sections.title: NIIT Calculation Example
sections.content:
**Single taxpayer:**
• MAGI: $280,000
• Net investment income: $50,000

**Step 1:** MAGI over threshold
$280,000 - $200,000 = $80,000

**Step 2:** Lesser amount
Lesser of $50,000 (NII) or $80,000 (excess MAGI) = $50,000

**Step 3:** Calculate NIIT
$50,000 × 3.8% = **$1,900**

sections.title: NIIT Planning Strategies
sections.content:
**To reduce NIIT:**

• Increase active participation in businesses (changes passive to active)
• Use tax-exempt investments
• Harvest capital losses to offset gains
• Time recognition of gains/income
• Invest in qualified opportunity zones (deferral)
• Convert passive rental to active (real estate professional status)

**NIIT applies to threshold, not total income** - some income escapes even for high earners!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Rental Income
sections.content:
**Rental income is usually subject to NIIT!**

Rental activities are generally passive, so rental income is usually Net Investment Income.

**Exception:** Real estate professionals with:
• 750+ hours of material participation
• More than 50% of services in real property trades

**Their rental income may be ACTIVE and escape NIIT!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NIIT is 3.8% tax on investment income above thresholds
Thresholds: $200,000 single / $250,000 MFJ
Applies to lesser of NII or excess MAGI
Includes: interest, dividends, passive income, capital gains
Excludes: wages, SE income, retirement distributions
Rental income usually included (passive activity)
Reported on Form 8960

knowledgeCheck.question: John, a single taxpayer, has Net Investment Income of $60,000 and a Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) of $220,000. Assuming the single filing status threshold for NIIT is $200,000, what is the amount of Net Investment Income Tax John owes?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The NIIT is calculated as 3.8% multiplied by the LESSER of (1) net investment income ($60,000) OR (2) the amount by which MAGI exceeds the threshold ($220,000 - $200,000 = $20,000). Therefore, 3.8% * $20,000 = $7,600.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to use the lesser of Net Investment Income and the excess MAGI to calculate the tax.

reveal.question: List four types of income that are typically included in Net Investment Income.
reveal.answer:
The following are included in Net Investment Income:
1. Interest
2. Dividends (qualified and ordinary)
3. Capital gains (including from home sale if over exclusion)
4. Rental and royalty income (if passive)
5. Passive business income
6. Annuity distributions (taxable portion)

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is EXCLUDED from Net Investment Income when calculating the Net Investment Income Tax?
options: Taxable interest income
Capital gains from the sale of stock
Wages from employment
Rental income from a passively managed property
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Wages and self-employment income are specifically excluded from the calculation of Net Investment Income. The other options listed are generally included.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider what types of income are derived from investments versus active work.

title: Additional Medicare Tax
description: Master the 0.9% Additional Medicare Tax on high earners

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: High earners face an extra 0.9% Medicare tax on wages and self-employment income above certain thresholds. Unlike regular Medicare tax, this has no employer match and applies only to individuals.

sections.title: Additional Medicare Tax Basics
sections.content:
**The Tax:**
• Additional 0.9% on earned income above thresholds
• On TOP of regular 1.45% Medicare tax
• No employer match (employee only)
• Created by ACA (same as NIIT)

**Applies to:**
• Wages (W-2 income)
• Self-employment income
• Railroad retirement (RRTA) compensation

sections.title: Employer Withholding Rules
sections.content:
**Employers must withhold:**
• Additional 0.9% on wages over $200,000
• Regardless of filing status!
• Regardless of spouse's income!

**This creates problems for:**
• MFJ couples where each earns under $200K but combined exceeds $250K
• They may owe additional tax at filing

• MFS filers with income over $125K but under $200K
• They may owe additional tax at filing

**Solution:** Request additional withholding on Form W-4

sections.title: Calculation Example
sections.content:
**Married Filing Jointly:**
• Spouse A wages: $180,000
• Spouse B wages: $120,000
• Total wages: $300,000

**Additional Medicare Tax:**
$300,000 - $250,000 (threshold) = $50,000
$50,000 × 0.9% = **$450**

**Withholding Problem:**
• Neither employer withheld (each under $200K)
• Full $450 owed at tax filing!

sections.title: Combined with Self-Employment
sections.content:
**Wages and SE income are combined:**

**Example:**
• W-2 wages: $150,000
• SE income: $100,000
• Total: $250,000

**Single taxpayer, $200,000 threshold:**
$250,000 - $200,000 = $50,000 subject to 0.9%
$50,000 × 0.9% = $450

**Key:** Allocate to SE income first if both wages and SE exist, for Form SE calculations.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Additional 0.9% Medicare tax on earned income above thresholds
Thresholds: $200,000 single / $250,000 MFJ / $125,000 MFS
No employer match - employee only tax
Employers withhold at $200,000 regardless of filing status
Combines wages and SE income to determine if threshold exceeded
May owe additional tax at filing if withholding insufficient
Reported on Form 8959

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, filing as Single, earns $210,000 in wages. Her employer withheld regular Medicare tax and the Additional Medicare Tax. Based on this information, what is the total Medicare tax rate applied to the $10,000 exceeding the $200,000 threshold?
options: It depends on Sarah's other sources of income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Additional Medicare Tax is 0.9% on top of the regular 1.45% Medicare tax. Therefore, the total rate applied to earnings exceeding the $200,000 threshold, in this scenario, is 2.35%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the Additional Medicare Tax is in addition to the regular Medicare tax.

reveal.question: What is the employer's threshold for withholding the Additional Medicare Tax from an employee's wages?
reveal.answer: Employers must withhold the Additional Medicare Tax of 0.9% from wages exceeding $200,000, regardless of the employee's filing status or other income.

knowledgeCheck.question: John and Mary are married and filing jointly. John earns $140,000, and Mary earns $120,000. Their combined income is $260,000. Which of the following statements is correct regarding the Additional Medicare Tax?
options: Neither John nor Mary will owe Additional Medicare Tax because their individual incomes are below $200,000.
John will owe Additional Medicare Tax on $60,000 ($200,000 threshold).
They will owe Additional Medicare Tax on $10,000 (combined income over the $250,000 threshold for MFJ).
Their employers will withhold the Additional Medicare Tax from both John and Mary's wages.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While their employers will only withhold the Additional Medicare Tax on an employee's wages exceeding $200,000, the *liability* for Additional Medicare Tax is based on the taxpayer's AGI. Married Filing Jointly filers have a $250,000 AGI threshold. Therefore, John and Mary will owe Additional Medicare Tax on the amount exceeding $250,000, which is $10,000 ($260,000 - $250,000).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider both individual income and the combined income threshold for MFJ filers.

title: Tax Rates and Tax Tables
description: Understand the graduated tax rate structure and how to compute tax liability

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Understanding the progressive tax system is fundamental. Know how marginal rates work, and you'll never confuse a client about whether earning more money actually costs them more than they earn!

sections.title: Marginal vs. Effective Rate
sections.content:
**Marginal Rate:**
The rate on your LAST dollar of income - your tax bracket.

**Effective Rate:**
Total tax divided by total income - your average rate.

**Example (Single, $100,000 taxable income):**
• Marginal rate: 22%
• Tax: $1,160 + $4,266 + $11,647.38 = $17,073
• Effective rate: ~17.1%

**Key:** Moving into a higher bracket only affects income IN that bracket!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: MFJ brackets are generally double Single brackets (except 35%/37%)
Marginal rate applies only to income in that bracket
Effective rate is always lower than marginal rate
Tax tables used for income under $100,000

title: Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT)
description: Understand the parallel AMT system and who it affects

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The AMT is a parallel tax system designed to ensure high-income taxpayers pay a minimum amount. While TCJA reduced AMT impact, it still affects some taxpayers, especially with large ISO exercises or significant state tax deductions.

sections.title: How AMT Works
sections.content:
**Two-step process:**

1. Calculate regular tax liability
2. Calculate tentative minimum tax (TMT)

**AMT = TMT - Regular Tax** (if positive)

**2024 AMT Rates:**
• 26% on first $232,600 (MFJ) of AMTI above exemption
• 28% on AMTI above that amount

**2024 Exemption Amounts:**
• Single/HOH: $85,700
• MFJ: $133,300
• MFS: $66,650

sections.title: AMT Preference Items
sections.content:
**Add to regular taxable income:**

• Private activity bond interest (tax-exempt for regular tax)
• Percentage depletion in excess of basis
• Pre-1987 accelerated depreciation

**Important for ISO:**
Exercising incentive stock options creates AMT income equal to the bargain element (FMV - exercise price), even though no regular income is recognized.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AMT ensures minimum tax is paid by high-income taxpayers
AMT rates are 26% and 28%
SALT deduction not allowed for AMT
ISO exercise is a major AMT trigger
TCJA increased exemptions, reducing AMT impact
AMT credit may be available for future years

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer has a regular tax liability of $30,000 and a tentative minimum tax (TMT) of $45,000. What is the amount of Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT) they owe?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The AMT is calculated as the Tentative Minimum Tax (TMT) minus the Regular Tax. In this case, $45,000 (TMT) - $30,000 (Regular Tax) = $15,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: AMT = TMT - Regular Tax (if positive)

reveal.question: What are the two AMT rates for 2024, and at what AMTI level does the higher rate apply for a Married Filing Jointly taxpayer?
reveal.answer: The AMT rates for 2024 are 26% and 28%. The 28% rate applies to AMTI above $232,600 for Married Filing Jointly taxpayers.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is an AMT preference item that is added back to regular taxable income when calculating AMTI?
options: State and local income taxes
Medical expenses exceeding 7.5% of AGI
Private activity bond interest (tax-exempt for regular tax)
Charitable contributions
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Private activity bond interest that is tax-exempt for regular tax purposes is an AMT preference item and must be added back to regular taxable income when calculating AMTI.
