knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about items that receive favorable treatment under regular tax but not under AMT.

title: Withholding Requirements
description: Master Form W-4 and employer withholding requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Proper withholding prevents underpayment penalties and surprise tax bills. Understanding Form W-4 options helps you advise clients on optimizing their withholding throughout the year.

sections.title: Form W-4 Overview
sections.content:
**Purpose:** Employee provides information to employer for determining withholding amount.

**When Required:**
• Starting new job
• Life changes affecting withholding (marriage, divorce, children)
• Want to adjust withholding amount

**Current Form (2020+):**
• Removed allowances
• Uses filing status and adjustments
• Multiple jobs/working spouse accounting
• Optional deduction and credit adjustments

sections.title: W-4 Key Sections
rows: For accuracy with multiple income sources

sections.title: Multiple Jobs / Working Spouse
sections.content:
**Step 2 Options:**

**Option A:** Use IRS Tax Withholding Estimator (online)

**Option B:** Multiple Jobs Worksheet
• For two similar-paying jobs
• Helps calculate additional withholding

**Option C:** Check box (simplest)
• For two jobs with similar pay
• May slightly over-withhold

**Privacy:** Higher earner can claim all adjustments on their W-4; lower earner claims nothing.

sections.title: Backup Withholding
sections.content:
**Rate: 24%**

**Applied when:**
• Payee doesn't provide TIN
• IRS notifies payer of incorrect TIN
• Payee underreported interest/dividends

**Applies to:**
• Interest payments
• Dividends
• Nonemployee compensation (1099-NEC)
• Other 1099 payments

**NOT on wages** - those use regular withholding

sections.title: Exempt from Withholding
sections.content:
**Can claim exempt if:**

• Last year: Owed no tax AND got refund of all tax withheld
• This year: Expect same situation

**To claim exempt:**
• Write "Exempt" on Form W-4 line (Step 4(c) area)
• Must submit new W-4 by February 15 each year

**Note:** Claiming exempt doesn't mean income isn't taxable - just that no withholding will occur!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: W-4 Changes
sections.content:
**New W-4 is NOT required for:**
• Tax law changes (employer adjusts tables)
• New tax year (unless claiming exempt)

**Old W-4s remain valid** unless employee makes changes or claims exempt.

**Employer responsibilities:**
• Cannot advise on how to complete W-4
• Must process as submitted
• Cannot reject as "too many dependents"

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Form W-4 determines wage withholding amount
No more allowances - uses filing status and adjustments
Multiple jobs require Step 2 adjustments for accuracy
Backup withholding is 24% when TIN issues exist
Can claim exempt if no tax liability expected
Exempt claims must be renewed annually by Feb 15
Old W-4s remain valid unless employee changes

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios requires an employee to submit a new Form W-4 to their employer?
options: The employee receives a small raise.
The employee's spouse starts a second part-time job with similar pay.
The employee pays off their mortgage.
The employee moves to a different state.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Starting a second job with similar pay significantly impacts the overall tax liability and requires an adjustment to withholding. A raise, paying off a mortgage, or moving to a different state don't directly change the information required on Form W-4 for withholding purposes.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider events that directly impact the number of jobs or dependents the employee has.

reveal.question: What is the backup withholding rate?
reveal.answer: The backup withholding rate is 24%.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Withholds income taxes from wages
Withholds taxes due to taxpayer non-compliance

rows.label: Form Used
values: No specific form, triggered by IRS notification or missing TIN

rows.label: Income Types Subject To
values: Wages, salaries, other compensation
Interest, dividends, non-employee compensation (1099-NEC), other 1099 payments

rows.label: Rate
values: Varies based on W-4 and income

title: Child Tax Credit and ODC
description: Master the CTC, ACTC, and Credit for Other Dependents

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Child Tax Credit provides substantial benefits to families. Understanding who qualifies, the refundable vs. nonrefundable portions, and how the Credit for Other Dependents works is essential for serving families with children.

sections.title: Qualifying Child Requirements for CTC
sections.content:
**The child must:**

1. Be under age **17** at end of tax year
2. Be your **qualifying child** (relationship, residency, support tests)
3. Have a valid **Social Security Number** (not ITIN or ATIN)
4. Be claimed as **your dependent**
5. Be a U.S. citizen, national, or resident alien
6. NOT file a joint return (unless only for refund)

**SSN must be issued BEFORE return due date** (including extensions)

sections.title: Phase-Out Rules
sections.content:
**Phase-out thresholds:**

• **MFJ:** $400,000
• **All others:** $200,000

**How it works:**
• Credit reduced by $50 for each $1,000 (or fraction) over threshold
• Both CTC and ODC subject to same phase-out

**Example:**
Single with MAGI of $210,000:
• Over threshold by $10,000
• Reduction: 10 × $50 = $500
• One child: $2,000 - $500 = **$1,500 CTC**

sections.title: Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC)
sections.content:
**The refundable portion:**

If CTC exceeds tax liability, ACTC provides refund.

**Calculation:**
15% × (Earned income - $2,500) = Potential ACTC

**Maximum:** $1,700 per qualifying child (2024)

**Example:**
• Earned income: $30,000
• Tax liability: $500
• CTC: $2,000 (one child)
• Nonrefundable portion used: $500
• Remaining: $1,500
• ACTC = 15% × ($30,000 - $2,500) = $4,125 → Limited to $1,500 (or max $1,700)

sections.title: Credit for Other Dependents (ODC)
sections.content:
**$500 credit for dependents who don't qualify for CTC:**

**Examples:**
• Children age 17 or older
• Children with ITIN (no SSN)
• Parents claimed as dependents
• Other qualifying relatives

**Nonrefundable only** - can reduce tax to zero but not below

**Same phase-out as CTC applies**

sections.title: ⚠️ Due Diligence Requirements
sections.content:
**Paid preparers must:**

• Complete Form 8867 due diligence checklist
• Keep records for 3 years
• Ask questions if information seems incorrect
• Know the eligibility rules

**Penalty: $635 per failure (2024)**

CTC/ACTC is one of the four credits requiring due diligence!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CTC: $2,000 per qualifying child under 17 with SSN
ACTC: Refundable portion up to $1,700 per child
ODC: $500 for dependents who don't qualify for CTC
Phase-out: $200,000 (single) / $400,000 (MFJ)
Child MUST have SSN for CTC (ITIN = ODC only)
Due diligence required - $635 penalty for failures
ACTC based on 15% of earned income over $2,500

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals CANNOT be claimed for the Child Tax Credit (CTC)?
options: A 16-year-old child with a valid Social Security number who lives with their parents and is claimed as their dependent.
A 15-year-old child with an ITIN who lives with their parents and is claimed as their dependent.
A 10-year-old child with a valid Social Security number who lives with their grandparents, who provide all their support and claim them as a dependent.
A 12-year-old U.S. citizen with a valid Social Security number who lives with their parents and is claimed as their dependent.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The child with an ITIN cannot be claimed for the CTC. A valid Social Security number is required.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the Social Security Number requirement.

reveal.question: What is the Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) threshold for the Child Tax Credit phase-out for taxpayers who are Married Filing Jointly (MFJ)?
reveal.answer: The MAGI threshold for MFJ taxpayers is $400,000.

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer has earned income of $15,000 and a tax liability of $300. They are eligible for a $2,000 Child Tax Credit for one qualifying child. What is the amount of the Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC) they can receive?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The ACTC is calculated as 15% * (Earned Income - $2,500). In this case, 15% * ($15,000 - $2,500) = $1,875. Since the maximum ACTC per qualifying child is $1,700 (in 2024), the taxpayer receives the smaller of the two, which is not applicable in this case.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: 15% * (Earned Income - $2,500), and compare the result to the maximum ACTC.

rows.label: Nature
values: Nonrefundable (initially)
Refundable

rows.label: Relationship to Tax Liability
values: Reduces tax liability
Provides a refund if CTC exceeds tax liability

rows.label: Calculation Base
values: Up to $2,000 per qualifying child
15% of (Earned Income - $2,500), up to $1,700 per qualifying child (2024)

rows.label: Purpose
values: Reduce overall tax burden
Provide assistance to low-income families

title: Child Tax Credit
description: Master the child tax credit and credit for other dependents

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Child Tax Credit is one of the most valuable family credits. Understanding who qualifies, the refundable portion, and income limits helps maximize benefits for clients with children.

sections.title: Qualifying Child for CTC
sections.content:
**Must meet ALL of these:**

• Under age 17 at end of tax year
• Your qualifying child (per dependency rules)
• U.S. citizen, national, or resident alien
• Claimed as YOUR dependent
• Has valid SSN (not ITIN)

**Key:** Must have SSN by return due date (including extensions)

sections.title: Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC)
sections.content:
**The refundable portion:**

Up to $1,700 per qualifying child (2024)

**Calculated as:**
15% of earned income over $2,500

**Example:**
• Earned income: $20,000
• Calculation: ($20,000 - $2,500) × 15% = $2,625
• Limited to $1,700 per child

**Three qualifying children or more:**
Alternative calculation using excess FICA taxes may apply.

sections.title: Credit for Other Dependents
sections.content:
**$500 nonrefundable credit for:**

• Dependents age 17+
• Dependents who don't have SSN (ITIN holders)
• Parents and other non-child dependents
• Children under 17 with only ITIN

**Same AGI phase-out as CTC applies**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: ITIN vs SSN
sections.content:
**Child with ITIN = $500 Credit for Other Dependents**
**Child with SSN = $2,000 CTC**

**Parent has ITIN but child has SSN:**
Full $2,000 CTC allowed!

**Key:** It's the CHILD'S number that matters for the credit amount.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CTC is $2,000 per qualifying child under 17
ACTC (refundable portion) limited to $1,700 per child
Child must have valid SSN for full CTC
Credit for Other Dependents is $500 for non-CTC dependents
Phase-out begins at $200,000 ($400,000 MFJ)
ITIN holders get $500 credit, not full CTC

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals qualifies for the Child Tax Credit (CTC)?
options: A 16-year-old child who is a U.S. resident alien and has an Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN).
A 17-year-old child who is a U.S. citizen and claimed as a dependent.
A 15-year-old child who is a U.S. citizen, claimed as a dependent, and has a Social Security Number (SSN).
A 14-year-old child who is a U.S. national but not claimed as a dependent.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is a 15-year-old child who is a U.S. citizen, claimed as a dependent, and has an SSN. To qualify for the CTC, the child must be under 17, a U.S. citizen, national, or resident alien, claimed as a dependent, and have a valid SSN.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember all conditions must be met. Consider the age and SSN requirements.

reveal.question: How is the Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC) generally calculated?
reveal.answer: The ACTC is generally calculated as 15% of earned income exceeding $2,500, up to a maximum of $1,700 per qualifying child for 2024. Remember this amount may change from year to year.

sections.title: CTC vs. Credit for Other Dependents

rows.label: Qualifying Individual
values: Child under 17 with SSN
Dependents age 17+, ITIN holders, or non-child dependents

rows.label: Credit Amount
values: Up to $2,000 per child (subject to limitations)
$500 per qualifying individual

rows.label: Refundable
values: Portion may be refundable (ACTC)
Nonrefundable

title: Child and Dependent Care Credit
description: Learn the rules for claiming the dependent care credit

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Working parents need care for their children. This credit helps offset those costs, but has specific rules about qualifying expenses and the work requirement.

sections.title: Credit Calculation
sections.content:
**Credit = Applicable Percentage × Qualified Expenses**

**Maximum Qualified Expenses:**
• $3,000 for one qualifying person
• $6,000 for two or more

**Applicable Percentage:**
• 35% for AGI ≤ $15,000
• Reduces by 1% for each $2,000 over $15,000
• Floors at 20% for AGI over $43,000

**Maximum Credit:**
• $1,050 (one qualifying person) at 35%
• $2,100 (two or more) at 35%
• $600/$1,200 at 20% (most taxpayers)

sections.title: Qualifying Person
sections.content:
**Care must be for:**

1. Your dependent under age 13
   - Must be your qualifying child
   - Must have lived with you more than half the year

2. Your disabled spouse
   - Physically or mentally incapable of self-care
   - Lived with you more than half the year

3. Any disabled dependent
   - Any age if disabled
   - Must be your dependent

sections.title: Work Requirement
sections.content:
**Both spouses must have earned income** (MFJ)

**Exception - Deemed earned income:**
• Full-time student: $250/month (one dependent) or $500 (two+)
• Disabled spouse: Same deemed amounts

**Expense limitation:**
Cannot claim more than the lower-earning spouse's income.

sections.title: Qualifying Expenses
rows: Payments to person you claim as dependent

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Credit is 20%-35% of up to $3,000/$6,000 expenses
Must have earned income (both spouses if MFJ)
Qualifying person must be under 13 (or disabled)
Day camp qualifies; overnight camp does not
Cannot pay your own dependent under 19
Employer DCAP reduces expense limit dollar-for-dollar

knowledgeCheck.question: Mark and Sarah are married and file jointly. Their adjusted gross income (AGI) is $45,000. They paid $7,000 in childcare expenses for their two children, ages 6 and 8, so that Sarah could work. Sarah earned $25,000 and Mark earned $10,000. What is the amount of qualifying expenses Mark and Sarah can use to calculate the child and dependent care credit?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The maximum qualified expenses for two or more qualifying persons is $6,000. While they paid $7,000, they are limited to the maximum amount. The lower-earning spouse's income ($10,000) is also a limitation, but it does not affect the expenses in this case since it is higher than $6000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the maximum expense limits and the lower-earning spouse rule.

reveal.question: What is the applicable percentage for the Child and Dependent Care Credit if the taxpayer's AGI is $25,000?
reveal.answer: The applicable percentage starts at 35% for AGI up to $15,000. It then decreases by 1% for each $2,000 (or fraction thereof) over $15,000.  Therefore, for an AGI of $25,000, the reduction is 5% (($25,000-$15,000)/$2,000 = 5).  The applicable percentage is 35% - 5% = 30%.

rows.label: Age Requirement
values: Under 13
None (Must be disabled)

rows.label: Self-Care Ability
values: Must require care to enable you to work or look for work.
Must be physically or mentally incapable of self-care.

rows.label: Residency Requirement
values: Must live with you for more than half the year.
Must live with you for more than half the year.

knowledgeCheck.question: John and Mary are married and file jointly. Mary is a full-time student. They have one qualifying child. What is the amount of Mary's deemed earned income for each month to calculate the Child and Dependent Care Credit?
options: Mary must have actual earned income to qualify for the credit.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A full-time student is deemed to have earned $250 per month if there is one qualifying person. If there were two or more qualifying persons, the deemed earned income would be $500 per month.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the deemed earned income amounts for full-time students.

title: Education Credits
description: Master the American Opportunity Credit and Lifetime Learning Credit

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Education credits can be worth thousands! Know which credit is better for each situation - they're mutually exclusive per student per year.

sections.title: American Opportunity Credit (AOTC)
sections.content:
**Credit Calculation:**
100% of first $2,000 + 25% of next $2,000 = **$2,500 max**

**Requirements:**
• First 4 years of postsecondary education
• Pursuing degree or credential
• At least half-time enrollment
• No felony drug conviction

**2024 Income Phase-out:**
• Single: $80,000 - $90,000
• MFJ: $160,000 - $180,000

**40% Refundable:** Up to $1,000 can be refunded even with $0 tax

sections.title: Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC)
sections.content:
**Credit Calculation:**
20% of up to $10,000 expenses = **$2,000 max**

**Key differences from AOTC:**
• Any year of study (not just first 4)
• One course qualifies (no half-time requirement)
• Career improvement courses qualify
• NOT refundable

**2024 Income Phase-out:**
• Single: $80,000 - $90,000
• MFJ: $160,000 - $180,000

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: AOTC vs LLC Choice
sections.content:
**Can only claim ONE credit per student per year!**

**Choose AOTC when:**
• First 4 years of college
• Would benefit from refundable portion
• Student enrolled at least half-time

**Choose LLC when:**
• Graduate school
• Fifth year or beyond
• Part-time or single course
• Career improvement (not degree)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AOTC: $2,500 max, 40% refundable, first 4 years only
LLC: $2,000 max, nonrefundable, unlimited years
Both phase out at same AGI levels ($80K-$90K single)
AOTC requires half-time enrollment; LLC does not
Books qualify for AOTC even if not paid to school
Cannot claim MFS for either credit

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayers, John and Mary, have $12,000 in qualified education expenses for their daughter, Sarah, who is in her second year of college pursuing a degree full-time. Their modified adjusted gross income (MAGI) is within the AOTC income limits. Assuming all other AOTC requirements are met, what is the maximum amount of American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC) they can claim?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The AOTC is calculated as 100% of the first $2,000 in qualified expenses plus 25% of the next $2,000. Therefore, the maximum AOTC is $2,000 + ($2,000 * 0.25) = $2,500. The total expenses exceed the amount used for the maximum credit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the calculation formula for AOTC and the maximum credit amount.

reveal.question: List four requirements for a student to qualify for the American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC).
reveal.answer: 1. The student must be in their first four years of postsecondary education. 2. The student must be pursuing a degree or other credential. 3. The student must be enrolled at least half-time. 4. The student must not have a felony drug conviction.

rows.label: Refundable
values: Yes (up to 40% or $1,000)
No

rows.label: Years of Study
values: First 4 years of postsecondary education
Any year of study

rows.label: Enrollment Requirement
values: At least half-time
One course qualifies

rows.label: Career Improvement Courses
values: No
Yes

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah is taking a single computer course to improve her job skills. The course costs $3,000. Assuming she meets all other requirements and her income is within the phase-out range, what is the maximum amount of Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC) that she can claim?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Lifetime Learning Credit is 20% of up to $10,000 in educational expenses. In this case, 20% of $3,000 is $600. Even though the maximum credit is $2,000, the credit is limited to 20% of the actual expenses.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the LLC calculation: 20% of qualified expenses, up to a maximum expense of $10,000.

title: Premium Tax Credit
description: Understand the health insurance marketplace credit and reconciliation

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Premium Tax Credit helps individuals afford health insurance through the Marketplace. Understanding how advance payments work and the reconciliation process is essential for accurate tax returns.

sections.title: PTC Basics
sections.content:
**What is the PTC?**
A refundable credit to help pay for health insurance purchased through the Health Insurance Marketplace (healthcare.gov).

**How it works:**
• Calculate annual PTC based on income and family size
• Can receive advance payments (APTC) monthly to reduce premiums
• Reconcile at tax time - may owe back or get more

**Cannot have access to:**
• Employer-sponsored coverage that's affordable/adequate
• Medicare, Medicaid, CHIP
• TRICARE, VA coverage

sections.title: Income Requirements
sections.content:
**Household income between:**
• 100% - 400% of Federal Poverty Level (FPL)

**2024 FPL Reference:**
• 100% FPL (single): ~$14,580
• 400% FPL (single): ~$58,320
• Add ~$5,380 per additional household member

**Special rule extended through 2025:**
No cliff at 400% FPL - credit phases out smoothly above 400%

sections.title: Reconciliation Process
sections.content:
**At tax time, compare:**
• Advance PTC received during year
• Actual PTC based on year-end income

**If actual > advance:**
Claim additional credit = refund increase

**If advance > actual:**
Must repay excess (subject to caps based on income)

**Form 8962 required** for anyone who:
• Received advance payments, or
• Is claiming the PTC

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: PTC helps pay for Marketplace health insurance
Income must be 100-400% FPL (extended cliff removal through 2025)
APTC reduces monthly premiums in advance
Form 8962 reconciles advance payments with actual eligibility
May owe back excess APTC (repayment caps apply)
MFS can now claim PTC in limited circumstances

knowledgeCheck.question: A single taxpayer with no dependents has an annual household income of $60,000 in 2024. They purchased health insurance through the Marketplace and received advance payments of the Premium Tax Credit (APTC). Based solely on the information provided, which of the following is TRUE regarding their eligibility for the PTC?
options: They are ineligible for the PTC because their income exceeds 400% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL).
They are eligible for the PTC, and the credit will phase out smoothly as their income is above the standard 400% FPL threshold.
They are eligible for the PTC, but their APTC will be significantly reduced due to their income level.
They are eligible for the PTC, provided they meet other requirements, but will have to repay all of the APTC they received due to their income level.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The special rule extending beyond 2025 eliminates the cliff at 400% FPL. The credit phases out smoothly above this threshold. Therefore, the taxpayer is still potentially eligible, even though their income exceeds the standard 400% FPL.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the special rule extending the PTC beyond the normal income limits.

reveal.question: Explain the difference between Advance Premium Tax Credit (APTC) and the Actual Premium Tax Credit.
reveal.answer: APTC is the estimated amount of the Premium Tax Credit paid in advance throughout the year to lower your monthly health insurance premiums. The Actual Premium Tax Credit is the final amount of the credit you are eligible for, calculated based on your actual income and household size at the end of the tax year. This is determined when you file Form 8962.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios requires a taxpayer to file Form 8962, Premium Tax Credit (PTC)?
options: The taxpayer purchased health insurance through the Marketplace but did not receive any advance payments of the PTC.
The taxpayer's income was below 100% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL) and they received advance payments of the PTC.
The taxpayer purchased health insurance outside of the Marketplace and received a Form 1095-B.
The taxpayer received advance payments of the PTC during the year, regardless of whether their actual income matched the initial estimate.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Form 8962 is required for anyone who received advance payments of the Premium Tax Credit (APTC) during the year to reconcile the APTC with the actual PTC they are eligible for based on their year-end income. Even if the taxpayer's income was accurate, the form is still required.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the scenario where advance payments were received.

title: Retirement Savings Contributions Credit
description: Learn about the Saver's Credit for low-to-moderate income retirement contributions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Saver's Credit (officially Retirement Savings Contributions Credit) provides extra incentive for low-to-moderate income workers to save for retirement. It's often overlooked!

sections.title: How the Credit Works
sections.content:
**Credit = Rate × Contribution (up to $2,000)**

**Maximum credit:**
• $1,000 per person ($2,000 MFJ)

**Eligible contributions:**
• Traditional and Roth IRA
• 401(k), 403(b), 457
• SIMPLE IRA
• ABLE accounts

**Must be age 18+, not a student, and not claimed as dependent**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Credit can be 10%, 20%, or 50% of contributions up to $2,000
Maximum credit is $1,000 per person
Must be 18+, not full-time student, not a dependent
Nonrefundable credit
AGI limits are relatively low - benefits lower-income savers

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals is MOST likely eligible for the Retirement Savings Contributions Credit (Saver's Credit)?
options: A 20-year-old college student who is claimed as a dependent on their parents' tax return and contributed $1,500 to a Roth IRA.
A 17-year-old who earned $5,000 at a summer job and contributed $1,000 to a traditional IRA.
A 35-year-old single individual with an adjusted gross income (AGI) of $25,000 who contributed $2,000 to a 401(k).
A 40-year-old married individual filing separately with an AGI of $40,000, who contributed $1,000 to a 403(b).
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The 35-year-old is most likely eligible. To be eligible, an individual must be age 18 or older, not a student, and not claimed as a dependent. The 20-year-old is a student and a dependent. The 17-year-old is not old enough. While we don't know the filing status AGI limits for the credit, MFJ limits are double single limits, and MFS limits are usually very low or not available. Therefore, the married filing separately individual is unlikely to qualify due to the high AGI.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the age, student, and dependency requirements.

reveal.question: What is the general formula for calculating the Retirement Savings Contributions Credit (Saver's Credit)?
reveal.answer: Credit = Rate × Contribution (up to $2,000 per person, $4,000 MFJ). Remember this only gives you the amount of the contribution that *could* be used for the credit. You must then calculate the AGI limits to determine which rate applies (0%, 10%, 20%, or 50%). The credit is nonrefundable and may be reduced by distributions received during the testing period.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following retirement savings plans is NOT eligible for the Retirement Savings Contributions Credit (Saver's Credit)?
options: A traditional IRA
A Roth IRA
A Health Savings Account (HSA)
A 403(b) plan
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Health Savings Accounts (HSAs) are not retirement savings plans and are not eligible for the Saver's Credit. Traditional IRAs, Roth IRAs, and 403(b) plans are all eligible.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on what constitutes a retirement savings plan.

title: Kiddie Tax Rules
description: Understand taxation of unearned income for children

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The kiddie tax prevents parents from shifting investment income to children in lower tax brackets. Understanding when it applies and how to calculate it is essential for family tax planning.

sections.title: Who is Subject to Kiddie Tax?
sections.content:
**The kiddie tax applies if:**

• Child has more than $2,500 (2024) of unearned income, AND
• Child meets age requirements:
  - Under age 18 at year end, OR
  - Age 18 with earned income ≤ 50% of support, OR
  - Age 19-23 and full-time student with earned income ≤ 50% of support

• At least one parent is alive at year end
• Child doesn't file joint return with spouse

sections.title: What is Unearned Income?
content: Interest and dividends
Capital gains
Rental income
Taxable Social Security benefits
Pension/annuity distributions
Income from trusts
Taxable scholarships (non-tuition portion)
items: **Interest**: Taxable interest from banks, bonds, loans.
**Dividends**: Ordinary and qualified dividends from stock.
**Capital Gains**: Net gain from sale of assets.
**Rents**: Passive income from property.
**Trust Distributions**: Unearned income distributed from trusts.
**Unemployment**: Unemployment compensation.

sections.title: Calculating the Kiddie Tax
sections.content:
**Form 8615 Calculation:**

1. Determine child's net unearned income
2. Calculate tax on net unearned income at parent's rate
3. Calculate tax on child's remaining taxable income at child's rate
4. Total tax = Sum of both

**Net Unearned Income =**
Unearned income - $1,250 - Greater of ($1,250 OR itemized deductions related to unearned income)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Parent's Rate
sections.content:
**The kiddie tax uses the parents' MARGINAL rate:**

• Add child's net unearned income to parents' taxable income
• Calculate incremental tax at that bracket
• This is the tax on the child's net unearned income

**If parents file MFS:** Use parent with higher taxable income

sections.title: Form 8814 Alternative
sections.content:
**Parents' Election to Report Child's Income:**

If child meets requirements, parents may include child's income on their return (Form 8814):

• Child's only income is interest/dividends
• Gross income < $12,500
• No estimated tax paid under child's SSN
• No withholding

**Disadvantage:** Additional $125 tax and child loses own standard deduction

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Kiddie tax applies to children under 18 (or 19-23 if student)
First $1,250 unearned income tax-free, next $1,250 at child's rate
Unearned income over $2,500 taxed at parents' marginal rate
Use Form 8615 to calculate kiddie tax
Parents may elect to report on their return using Form 8814
Earned income is always taxed at child's own rate

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios would NOT subject a child to the kiddie tax for the 2024 tax year?
options: A 17-year-old with $3,000 of dividend income and no earned income.
An 18-year-old with $1,000 of interest income and earned income that is 40% of their support.
A 20-year-old full-time student with $2,000 of capital gains and earned income that is 60% of their support.
A 22-year-old full-time student with $2,800 of rental income and no earned income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The kiddie tax does not apply because the 20-year-old's earned income is 60% of their support, exceeding the 50% threshold. The other scenarios meet the age and unearned income requirements.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Carefully review the age and support rules for the kiddie tax.

reveal.question: What is the standard deduction amount subtracted from a child's unearned income when calculating net unearned income for the kiddie tax in 2024?
reveal.answer: The standard deduction amount subtracted from unearned income in 2024 is $1,250. This amount is also used as the alternative amount to compare with the itemized deductions.

knowledgeCheck.question: A child has $4,000 of unearned income and no earned income. Assuming the child's parent is in the 22% tax bracket, what is the tax on the child's net unearned income based on the kiddie tax rules?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate net unearned income: $4,000 (unearned income) - $1,250 - $1,250 = $1,500. Then, calculate the tax at the parent's rate: $1,500 * 22% = $330.  Since the question asks about the tax on net *unearned* income, we do not need to calculate the child's tax rate on remaining income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the net unearned income before applying the parent's tax rate.

title: Estimated Tax Payments
description: Master the rules for quarterly estimated tax payments

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Clients with self-employment income, investment income, or other non-wage income must make estimated tax payments. Understanding safe harbors and penalty calculation helps clients avoid unnecessary penalties.

sections.title: Who Must Pay Estimated Tax?
sections.content:
**Required if:**

• Expected to owe $1,000 or more when return is filed, AND
• Withholding + credits will be less than the smaller of:
  - 90% of current year tax, OR
  - 100% of prior year tax (110% if prior year AGI > $150,000 MFJ)

**Not required if:**
• Owed zero tax in prior year (and was U.S. citizen/resident full year)

sections.title: Safe Harbor Rules
sections.content:
**No penalty if payments equal or exceed:**

**100% Safe Harbor:**
• Pay 100% of prior year tax liability
• Applies if prior year AGI ≤ $150,000 ($75,000 MFS)

**110% Safe Harbor:**
• Pay 110% of prior year tax liability
• Applies if prior year AGI > $150,000 ($75,000 MFS)

**90% Current Year:**
• Pay 90% of current year tax liability
• Always an alternative safe harbor

**Exception:** Prior year must have been 12 months and tax return filed

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Safe Harbor
sections.content:
**"Pay Last Year's Tax"**

• AGI ≤ $150K: Pay 100% of last year
• AGI > $150K: Pay 110% of last year

**OR pay 90% of this year (if you can predict it)**

**The 110% is the commonly tested one for high-income taxpayers!**

sections.title: Annualized Income Installment Method
sections.content:
**For Uneven Income:**

If income varies significantly during the year, taxpayer can use annualized income method to reduce earlier payments.

**How it works:**
• Calculate income for each period
• Annualize it (multiply by factor)
• Pay tax proportionate to income in each period

**Schedule AI (Form 2210)** required to claim this method and avoid/reduce penalty.

sections.title: Underpayment Penalty
sections.content:
**Form 2210 Calculation:**

• Penalty is essentially interest on late payments
• Rate is federal short-term rate + 3%
• Calculated from payment due date to earlier of:
  - Date payment made, or
  - Return due date (April 15)

**Penalty Waiver:**
Available if underpayment due to casualty, disaster, or other unusual circumstances
Retired/disabled taxpayers age 62+ in certain situations

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Estimated tax required if owing $1,000+ and withholding insufficient
Due dates: April 15, June 15, September 15, January 15
100% safe harbor if prior AGI ≤ $150K; 110% if higher
90% of current year tax is alternative safe harbor
Annualized income method helps with uneven income
Penalty = interest at federal short-term rate + 3%

knowledgeCheck.question: John and Mary are filing jointly. Their expected tax liability for the current year is $12,000. Their prior year tax liability was $10,000, and their Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) was $160,000. Their expected withholding and credits for the current year total $9,500. Are they required to make estimated tax payments?
options: No, because their withholding and credits exceed 90% of the current year's tax.
No, because their withholding and credits exceed 100% of the prior year's tax.
Yes, because their expected tax liability is over $1,000 and their withholding and credits are less than both 90% of the current year's tax and 110% of the prior year's tax.
Yes, because their AGI was over $150,000 in the prior year.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: They are required to make estimated tax payments. Their expected tax liability is over $1,000. We compare the withholding and credits ($9,500) to both 90% of the current year's tax (0.9 * $12,000 = $10,800) and 110% of the prior year's tax (1.1 * $10,000 = $11,000). Since $9,500 is less than both $10,800 and $11,000, estimated tax payments are required.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider the AGI threshold for the prior year tax safe harbor.

reveal.question: What are the two primary safe harbor rules that allow taxpayers to avoid estimated tax penalties, and what AGI thresholds apply to each (if any)?
reveal.answer:
1. **100% of Prior Year Tax Liability:** Pay 100% of the prior year's tax liability. This applies if the prior year's AGI was ≤ $150,000 (Married Filing Jointly) or $75,000 (Married Filing Separately).
2. **110% of Prior Year Tax Liability:** Pay 110% of the prior year's tax liability. This applies if the prior year's AGI was > $150,000 (Married Filing Jointly) or $75,000 (Married Filing Separately).
3. **90% of Current Year Tax Liability:** Pay 90% of the current year's tax liability. There is no AGI threshold for this rule.

sections.title: Safe Harbor: 100% vs. 110% of Prior Year Tax

rows.label: AGI Threshold (MFJ)
values: Prior year AGI ≤ $150,000
Prior year AGI > $150,000

rows.label: AGI Threshold (MFS)
values: Prior year AGI ≤ $75,000
Prior year AGI > $75,000

rows.label: Payment Required
values: Pay 100% of prior year's tax liability
Pay 110% of prior year's tax liability

title: Withholding Requirements
description: Understand income tax withholding and Form W-4

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Proper withholding is the first line of defense against underpayment penalties. Understanding how W-4 works helps clients adjust withholding to match their expected tax liability.

sections.title: Form W-4 Overview
sections.content:
**Current W-4 (2020+):**

• No more allowances/exemptions
• Based on actual tax credits and deductions
• Five steps (Steps 2-4 optional)

**Key Elements:**
• Filing status
• Multiple jobs/spouse works adjustment
• Dependents and credits
• Other adjustments (deductions, additional income)
• Extra withholding

sections.title: When New W-4 Required
sections.content:
**Employee must submit new W-4:**

• Within 10 days of changes that reduce withholding allowances
• Marriage or divorce
• New dependents
• Change in expected deductions

**Employee may (but not required to):**
• Submit new W-4 anytime
• Update for life changes

**Employer must:**
• Implement new W-4 by start of first payroll period ending ≥30 days after receipt

sections.title: Backup Withholding
sections.content:
**Rate: 24%**

**When required:**
• Payee didn't provide TIN (or incorrect TIN)
• IRS notifies payer that backup withholding is required
• Payee failed to certify not subject to backup withholding

**Applies to:**
• Interest
• Dividends
• Rents, royalties
• Non-employee compensation
• Broker transactions
• Other reportable payments

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Exempt from Withholding
sections.content:
**Employee can claim exempt from withholding if:**

• Had no tax liability last year, AND
• Expects no tax liability this year

**Exempt status expires February 15 of following year!**

Employee must submit new W-4 each year to continue exemption.

sections.title: Other Withholding Types
sections.content:
**Pensions and Annuities:**
• W-4P for periodic payments
• Can elect no withholding (with some exceptions)
• Default withholding if no W-4P

**Gambling Winnings:**
• 24% on winnings over threshold
• Generally $1,200 for slots/bingo, $1,500 for keno, $5,000 for other

**Supplemental Wages:**
• Flat 22% rate (or aggregate method)
• Includes bonuses, commissions, severance

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Form W-4 determines income tax withholding amount
2020+ W-4 uses tax credits and deductions, not allowances
New W-4 required within 10 days if circumstances reduce withholding
Backup withholding is 24% for missing/incorrect TIN
Exempt status expires February 15 each year
Supplemental wages: 22% flat rate

knowledgeCheck.question: John got married in June and his wife started a new, higher-paying job in August. They are considering updating their W-4 forms. Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding their W-4 filing requirements?
options: John is required to file a new W-4 within 10 days of the marriage because it reduces his withholding allowances.
John and his wife are required to file new W-4s within 10 days of his wife starting a new job.
John and his wife are not required to file new W-4s, but they may choose to do so to adjust their withholding.
John's wife is required to file a new W-4 within 10 days of starting a new job because it may affect their tax bracket.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Employees are not required to file a new W-4, but may choose to do so at any time. The new job for John's wife and marriage are life events that could impact their tax liability, making it a good idea to update the W-4, but it's not mandatory.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider whether the scenario involves a mandatory filing requirement or an optional adjustment.

reveal.question: What is the backup withholding rate, and what is one common trigger for backup withholding?
reveal.answer: The backup withholding rate is 24%. A common trigger is when a payee fails to provide their Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN) to the payer, or provides an incorrect TIN.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following types of payments is NOT subject to backup withholding?
options: Interest income
Dividend income
Wages paid to an employee
Non-employee compensation
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Backup withholding generally applies to investment-related income (interest, dividends), rents, royalties, non-employee compensation, and broker transactions. Wages paid to an employee are subject to regular income tax withholding, not backup withholding.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which types of income are typically reported on forms other than a W-2.

title: Premium Tax Credit
description: Understand the credit for health insurance purchased through the Marketplace

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Premium Tax Credit makes health insurance affordable for millions of Americans. Understanding eligibility, advance payments, and reconciliation is essential for clients who purchase coverage through Healthcare.gov.

sections.title: Eligibility Requirements
content: Purchased coverage through the Health Insurance Marketplace
Household income 100%-400% of Federal Poverty Level (no upper limit through 2025)
Cannot be claimed as dependent
Cannot file MFS (unless certain abuse/abandonment exceptions)
Not eligible for other minimum essential coverage (employer, Medicare, Medicaid)
items: **Relationship**: Must be child, stepchild, foster child, sibling, or descendant.
**Age**: Under 19, or under 24 if full-time student (or any age if disabled).
**Residency**: Lived with taxpayer > 1/2 of the year.
**Support**: Child did not provide > 1/2 of their own support.
**Joint Return**: Child cannot file a joint return (unless only for refund claim).

sections.title: How the Credit Works
sections.content:
**Credit Amount:**
Premium for second-lowest-cost Silver plan (SLCSP) minus expected contribution

**Expected Contribution:**
Based on household income as percentage of FPL, multiplied by applicable percentage

**2024 Applicable Percentages:**
• 100-150% FPL: 0% to 4% of income
• 150-200% FPL: 4% to 6.3% of income
• 200-250% FPL: 6.3% to 8.05% of income
• 250-400% FPL: 8.05% to 8.5% of income
• Over 400% FPL: 8.5% (due to ARP extension)

sections.title: Advance Premium Tax Credit (APTC)
sections.content:
**Paid in Advance:**
Based on estimated income from Marketplace application, credit is paid directly to insurer monthly.

**Must Reconcile:**
At tax time, compare actual income to estimate:
• If income higher → may owe back some APTC
• If income lower → may get additional credit

**Form 1095-A:**
Received from Marketplace showing:
• Monthly premiums
• Monthly SLCSP
• Monthly APTC paid

sections.title: Form 8962 Reconciliation
sections.content:
**Required if any APTC received or claiming PTC:**

**Part I:** Family size, modified AGI, FPL calculation
**Part II:** Monthly calculation (if circumstances changed)
**Part III:** Repayment limitation (if applicable)

**Repayment Caps (2024):**
Based on income, maximum repayment limited:
• Under 200% FPL: $375 (single) / $750 (other)
• 200-300% FPL: $950 (single) / $1,900 (other)
• 300-400% FPL: $1,600 (single) / $3,200 (other)
• Over 400% FPL: Full repayment required

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: MFS and PTC
sections.content:
**MFS taxpayers generally CANNOT claim PTC!**

**Limited Exception:**
• Victim of domestic abuse or spousal abandonment
• Living apart from spouse at end of year
• Indicate on Form 8962

**Key:** If client has APTC and files MFS, they may have to repay entire APTC!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: PTC available for Marketplace insurance if income 100%+ FPL
Credit = SLCSP premium minus expected contribution
APTC is paid in advance to insurer monthly
Must reconcile on Form 8962 using Form 1095-A data
Repayment caps exist for income under 400% FPL
MFS generally ineligible (limited exceptions)

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer's household income is 160% of the Federal Poverty Line (FPL). To calculate their expected contribution towards marketplace insurance, which of the following is TRUE regarding the applicable percentage used in the calculation?
options: The applicable percentage will always be 0%.
The applicable percentage will be a fixed percentage determined by the IRS each year.
The applicable percentage will fall between 0% and 4% of their income.
The applicable percentage will fall between 4% and a percentage determined by the IRS each year.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Taxpayers with household income between 150% and 200% of the FPL have an applicable percentage that falls between 4% and a percentage determined by the IRS each year. The specific percentage within that range will depend on their income within that range. The applicable percentage is used to determine the expected contribution towards the premium.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Review the income ranges and their corresponding applicable percentages.

sections.title: Form 1095-A: What Information Does it Provide?

reveal.question: What are the three key pieces of information provided on Form 1095-A that are needed to reconcile the Premium Tax Credit?
reveal.answer: 1. The monthly premium for the second-lowest cost Silver plan (SLCSP). 2. The total amount of advance payments of the Premium Tax Credit (APTC) paid to the insurance company. 3. Information about the individuals covered under the policy (e.g., names, social security numbers).

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer received APTC throughout the year based on an estimated income of $30,000. At the end of the year, their actual income was $40,000. Assuming all other eligibility requirements are met, what is the MOST LIKELY outcome when reconciling the Premium Tax Credit on Form 8962?
options: The taxpayer will receive an additional Premium Tax Credit.
The taxpayer will have no change to their tax liability.
The taxpayer will be required to repay some of the APTC.
The taxpayer will be required to repay all of the APTC.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since the taxpayer's actual income was higher than their estimated income, they likely received more APTC than they were entitled to. Therefore, they will likely be required to repay some of the APTC. The exact amount will depend on the applicable percentage for their income level.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that APTC is based on estimated income. If actual income is higher, you may owe back some of the credit.

title: Energy Credits for Individuals
description: Master the residential clean energy and efficiency credits

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Inflation Reduction Act significantly expanded energy credits! These credits can provide thousands of dollars for clients investing in clean energy and energy efficiency. Know the new rules!

sections.title: Residential Clean Energy Credit (25D)
sections.content:
**30% credit for:**

• Solar electric (photovoltaic) systems
• Solar water heaters
• Fuel cells
• Small wind energy systems
• Geothermal heat pumps
• Battery storage (3+ kWh capacity) - new for 2023+

**Must be for taxpayer's residence in U.S.**

**No dollar cap on credit amount!**

**Carryforward:** Unused credit carries forward indefinitely

sections.title: Energy Efficient Home Improvement Credit (25C)
sections.content:
**Annual Limits (2023+):**

**Overall Annual Cap: $3,200**

• $1,200 for insulation, windows, doors, home energy audits
• $2,000 for heat pumps, biomass stoves, heat pump water heaters

**Specific Limits:**
• Exterior doors: $250/door, $500 total
• Windows: $600 max
• Home energy audit: $150 max
• Insulation/air sealing: $1,200 max

**Must meet Energy Star requirements**

sections.title: Clean Vehicle Credit (30D)
sections.content:
**New for 2023+:**

Up to $7,500 for new qualifying EVs:
• $3,750 for battery component requirement
• $3,750 for critical mineral requirement

**Income Limits:**
• Single: $150,000 MAGI
• HOH: $225,000 MAGI
• MFJ: $300,000 MAGI

**Vehicle Price Caps:**
• SUVs, vans, pickups: $80,000
• Other vehicles: $55,000

**Transfer Option:** Can transfer credit to dealer at point of sale

sections.title: Used Clean Vehicle Credit (25E)
sections.content:
**New Credit for Used EVs:**

**Amount:** Lesser of $4,000 or 30% of sale price

**Requirements:**
• Model year at least 2 years before sale
• Price ≤ $25,000
• First transfer since 8/16/22
• Purchased from dealer

**Income Limits:**
• Single: $75,000 MAGI
• HOH: $112,500 MAGI
• MFJ: $150,000 MAGI

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Residential Clean Energy Credit: 30%, no cap, carryforward allowed
Home Improvement Credit: 30%, $3,200 annual cap, no carryforward
New EV credit up to $7,500, income and price limits apply
Used EV credit up to $4,000 or 30% of price
All credits are nonrefundable
Battery storage now qualifies for clean energy credit

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer Smith installed a solar water heater and a battery storage system (4 kWh capacity) for their primary residence in the U.S. in 2024. The total cost was $20,000. What is the amount of the Residential Clean Energy Credit Smith can claim?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Residential Clean Energy Credit (25D) is 30% of the cost of qualified expenses. In this case, 30% of $20,000 is $6,000. There is no dollar cap on this credit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the percentage and whether there is a limit.

rows.label: Credit Percentage/Amount
values: 30% of qualified expenses
Limited to specific dollar amounts for different improvements
Up to $7,500

rows.label: Overall Dollar Limit
values: None
$3,200 annually
Up to $7,500, but dependent on meeting battery and mineral requirements

rows.label: Income Limits
values: None
None
Yes: Single ($150,000 MAGI), HOH ($225,000 MAGI), MFJ ($300,000 MAGI)

rows.label: Property Type
values: Taxpayer's residence in the U.S.
Taxpayer's residence in the U.S.
New qualifying electric vehicle

sections.title: Energy Efficient Home Improvement Credit (25C) Question

knowledgeCheck.question: In 2024, a taxpayer spends $1,500 on a qualified home energy audit and $4,000 on a new heat pump for their home. What is the maximum amount of the Energy Efficient Home Improvement Credit (25C) they can claim?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The taxpayer can claim $150 for the home energy audit (limited to $150) and $2,000 for the heat pump (limited to $2,000). Therefore, the total credit is $150 + $2,000 = $2,150. The $3,200 overall limit is not reached.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the specific limits for each type of improvement before applying the overall annual limit.

title: Farm Income and Expenses
description: Understand special tax rules for farming operations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Farmers have unique tax rules and elections! Understanding Schedule F, income averaging, and special deductions helps you serve agricultural clients effectively.

sections.title: Schedule F Overview
sections.content:
**Who Files Schedule F:**
• Farmers (cultivating land, raising livestock)
• NOT ranchers who only buy/sell livestock (Schedule C)
• NOT agricultural laborers (Form W-2)

**Accounting Method:**
• Most farmers use cash method
• Can use accrual if desired
• Special rules for prepaid expenses

sections.title: Farm Income Averaging
sections.content:
**Schedule J Election:**

Farmers can elect to average current year farm income over prior 3 years.

**Benefits:**
• Smooth income fluctuations
• Lower marginal tax rate in high-income years
• Particularly helpful after good crop year

**Requirements:**
• Must have elected farm income
• Complete Schedule J
• Can use even if didn't farm in prior years

**Elected Farm Income:** Any portion of current year taxable income attributable to farming

sections.title: Crop Insurance Deferral
sections.content:
**Election to Defer:**

If crop insurance proceeds received because of destruction/damage to crops:
• Can elect to include in FOLLOWING year
• Must be cash-method farmer
• Would have reported crop income in following year under normal practice

**Applies to:**
• Crop insurance payments
• Disaster payments
• Weather-related livestock sales (different deferral period)

sections.title: Special Farm Deductions
sections.content:
**Unique to Farming:**

• Soil and water conservation expenses (limited)
• Fertilizer and lime (current or amortize)
• Land clearing (capitalize)
• Prepaid feed (limited deductions)

**Depreciation:**
• Farm buildings: 20-year MACRS
• Agricultural structures: 10 or 15 years
• Livestock: 3, 5, or 7 years
• Fencing: 7 or 15 years

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Schedule F for farming income and expenses
Most farmers use cash method accounting
Farm income averaging with Schedule J (3-year lookback)
Crop insurance can be deferred to following year
Livestock raised = ordinary income; purchased = Section 1231
Special depreciation rules for farm property

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals is MOST likely required to file Schedule F?
options: A rancher who purchases calves, raises them for six months, and then sells them for profit.
An agricultural laborer who is paid an hourly wage to harvest crops on a large farm.
A farmer who cultivates land and grows wheat as their primary source of income.
An individual who buys and sells livestock solely for resale.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Schedule F is used by farmers who cultivate land or raise livestock. The rancher who buys/sells livestock uses Schedule C. Agricultural laborers are employees and receive a W-2. The individual who buys and sells livestock is engaged in a business similar to the rancher and would use Schedule C.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the difference between raising livestock and simply buying/selling it.

reveal.question: What is the primary benefit of electing to use farm income averaging (Schedule J)?
reveal.answer: The primary benefit is to smooth out income fluctuations by averaging current-year farm income over the prior three years, potentially resulting in a lower marginal tax rate in high-income years.

knowledgeCheck.question: A cash-method farmer's wheat crop is destroyed by a hailstorm in Year 1. They receive crop insurance proceeds in December of Year 1. Under their normal business practice, they would have reported the wheat income in Year 2. Which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding the farmer's ability to defer the crop insurance proceeds?
options: The farmer MUST include the crop insurance proceeds in their Year 1 income.
The farmer can elect to include the crop insurance proceeds in their Year 2 income.
The farmer can only defer the crop insurance proceeds if they use the accrual method of accounting.
The farmer can only defer the crop insurance proceeds if the hailstorm was officially declared a federal disaster.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A cash-method farmer can elect to include crop insurance proceeds in the following year if the proceeds are received due to destruction/damage to crops, and they would have reported the crop income in the following year under normal practice.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the requirements for deferring crop insurance proceeds.

sections.title: Schedule F vs. Schedule C (Farm Context)

rows.label: Primary Activity
values: Cultivating land or raising livestock
Buying and selling livestock (resale)

rows.label: Eligible Taxpayers
values: Farmers
Ranchers (buying/selling livestock)

rows.label: Example
values: Growing corn or raising cattle from birth
Purchasing mature cattle and selling them shortly thereafter

title: Virtual Currency Transactions
description: Master the tax treatment of cryptocurrency and digital assets

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Cryptocurrency is everywhere! The IRS treats virtual currency as property, not currency. Understanding the tax implications of buying, selling, spending, and receiving crypto is increasingly important.

sections.title: Property Treatment
sections.content:
**Virtual currency is PROPERTY for tax purposes:**

• NOT currency (despite the name)
• General property transaction rules apply
• Every disposal is a taxable event
• Must track basis and holding period

**Examples of virtual currency:**
• Bitcoin, Ethereum, Litecoin
• Stablecoins
• NFTs (non-fungible tokens)
• Tokens received from airdrops

sections.title: Basis Determination
sections.content:
**Initial Basis:**
• Purchased: Cost in USD at time of purchase
• Received as income: FMV when received
• Gifted: Carryover basis (with modifications)
• Inherited: FMV at date of death

**Identifying Specific Units:**
• FIFO (First In, First Out) is default
• Specific identification allowed if:
  - Can identify specific unit sold
  - Documented before sale

**This matters because different purchases have different basis!**

sections.title: Reporting Requirements
sections.content:
**Form 1040 Question:**
Must answer yes/no to virtual currency question on Form 1040.

**Form 8949/Schedule D:**
• Report sales and exchanges
• Calculate gain/loss on each transaction
• Short-term vs. long-term classification

**Receiving as Income:**
• Report FMV as income when received
• Self-employment income if received for services
• May be subject to SE tax

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Crypto to Crypto
sections.content:
**Exchanging one cryptocurrency for another IS taxable!**

• Like-kind exchange (Section 1031) does NOT apply
• Must recognize gain/loss on each exchange
• New crypto takes FMV as basis

**Example:**
Trade 1 BTC (basis $10,000) for 10 ETH (FMV $50,000)
= $40,000 capital gain
New basis in ETH = $50,000

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Virtual currency is property, not currency
Every sale, exchange, or use is potentially taxable
Receiving crypto as income = ordinary income at FMV
Mining and staking rewards are ordinary income
Crypto-to-crypto trades are taxable (no like-kind exchange)
Must answer virtual currency question on Form 1040
Track basis carefully for each purchase/receipt

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding the tax treatment of virtual currency in the United States?
options: Virtual currency is treated as currency for all federal income tax purposes.
Virtual currency is treated as property for federal income tax purposes.
Virtual currency transactions are exempt from federal income tax reporting requirements if the amount is under $10,000.
The IRS has not yet issued guidance on the tax treatment of virtual currency.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The IRS treats virtual currency as property, not currency. This means general property transaction rules apply, requiring tracking of basis and reporting of gains/losses.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how the IRS generally treats assets like stocks or real estate.

reveal.question: How is the initial basis of virtual currency determined when it's received as income for services rendered?
reveal.answer: The initial basis is the fair market value (FMV) of the virtual currency at the time it is received. This FMV is also reported as income.

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah sells Bitcoin for $10,000 that she originally purchased for $2,000. Which form(s) is/are REQUIRED to report this transaction?
options: Form 1040 only.
Form 8949 and Schedule D only.
Form 1040, Form 8949, and Schedule D.
No forms are required as long as the gain is reinvested within 6 months.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The transaction must be reported on Form 8949 to calculate the gain/loss, which is then transferred to Schedule D to determine the overall capital gain/loss. The taxpayer must also answer the virtual currency question on Form 1040.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider all steps of reporting a sale of property.

title: Foreign Income and Exclusions
description: Understand tax rules for U.S. citizens with foreign income

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: U.S. citizens and residents are taxed on worldwide income! The foreign earned income exclusion (FEIE) can significantly reduce tax for those living and working abroad. Know the requirements!

sections.title: U.S. Worldwide Taxation
sections.content:
**U.S. citizens and residents are taxed on worldwide income:**

• Regardless of where earned
• Regardless of where they live
• Must report all foreign income
• May get relief through:
  - Foreign Earned Income Exclusion
  - Foreign Housing Exclusion/Deduction
  - Foreign Tax Credit

**FBAR:** Report foreign accounts over $10,000 aggregate

sections.title: Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE)
sections.content:
**2024 Exclusion: $126,500**

**Requirements:**
• U.S. citizen or resident alien
• Tax home in foreign country
• Meet either:
  - Bona Fide Residence Test, OR
  - Physical Presence Test

**Form 2555** to claim exclusion

**What Qualifies:**
• Wages, salaries, professional fees
• Self-employment income
• NOT: Pensions, investments, employer-paid housing

sections.title: Qualification Tests
rows: Uninterrupted period including full tax year
330 full days in foreign country in 12-month period

sections.title: Tax Home Requirement
sections.content:
**Tax home must be in foreign country:**

• Where principal place of business is located
• Cannot maintain significant ties to U.S. home
• Temporary work assignments may not shift tax home

**Abode in U.S.:**
If you maintain an abode (home) in the U.S., you must establish bona fide residence abroad to qualify for FEIE.

**Key:** Your tax home is where you WORK, not where your family lives.

sections.title: Foreign Housing Exclusion
sections.content:
**Additional Exclusion:**

Housing expenses over base amount, up to limit:

**Base Amount:** 16% of FEIE ($20,240 for 2024)
**Maximum Exclusion:** 30% of FEIE ($37,950 for 2024)

**Qualifying Housing Expenses:**
• Rent
• Utilities (except telephone)
• Insurance
• Furniture rental
• Repairs

**NOT Included:** Home purchase, improvements, lavish expenses

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: FEIE vs. Foreign Tax Credit
sections.content:
**Cannot Double Dip!**

• Cannot claim Foreign Tax Credit on income excluded under FEIE
• Must choose which benefit to use
• If income exceeds FEIE, can use FTC on excess

**Strategy:**
• High-tax countries: FTC may be better
• Low/no-tax countries: FEIE often better
• Many use both (FEIE on first $126,500, FTC on rest)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: U.S. taxes citizens/residents on worldwide income
FEIE: Up to $126,500 (2024) exclusion for foreign earned income
Must meet Bona Fide Residence OR Physical Presence test
Tax home must be in foreign country
Foreign Housing Exclusion available for housing costs over base amount
Foreign Tax Credit cannot be claimed on excluded income

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement to qualify for the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE)?
options: Being a U.S. citizen or resident alien.
Having a tax home in a foreign country.
Meeting the Bona Fide Residence Test or the Physical Presence Test.
Owning property in a foreign country.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Owning property in a foreign country is not a requirement for the FEIE. The requirements are: U.S. citizen or resident alien, tax home in a foreign country, and meeting either the Bona Fide Residence Test or the Physical Presence Test.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Review the specific requirements listed for the FEIE.

reveal.question: What is the maximum Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) amount for the 2024 tax year?
reveal.answer: The maximum FEIE amount for the 2024 tax year is $126,500.

sections.title: Bona Fide Residence Test vs. Physical Presence Test

rows.label: Definition
values: Requires establishing residency in a foreign country for an uninterrupted period that includes an entire tax year.
Requires being physically present in a foreign country or countries for at least 330 full days during any period of 12 consecutive months.

rows.label: Intent
values: Focuses on the taxpayer's intention to reside permanently in the foreign country.
Focuses solely on the number of days spent in a foreign country.

rows.label: Home Considerations
values: Maintaining a U.S. abode can make it more difficult to prove bona fide residence abroad.
The location of one's abode is less critical, as long as the physical presence requirements are met.

title: Due Diligence Requirements
description: Master preparer due diligence for refundable credits

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Due diligence isn't optional! Paid preparers face $635 penalties per credit/status if they fail to meet requirements. These penalties add up fast. This is heavily tested and affects your practice daily.

sections.title: Form 8867: Due Diligence Checklist
sections.content:
**Required for returns claiming:**

• Earned Income Credit (EIC)
• Child Tax Credit (CTC) / Additional CTC
• American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC)
• Head of Household filing status

**Form 8867 must be completed and retained for 3 years**

**Penalty: $635 per failure (2024)**

4 items on one return = potential $2,540 penalty!

sections.title: Four Due Diligence Requirements
rows: Know the law and how to apply it
Review documents and don't ignore inconsistencies
Ask questions when information seems incorrect/incomplete
Keep Form 8867, worksheets, and supporting docs for 3 years

sections.title: Knowledge Requirement
sections.content:
**You must know the eligibility rules for:**

• EIC (earned income, investment income limit, residency, relationship)
• CTC (qualifying child, age, support, residency)
• AOTC (enrollment status, expenses, eligible institution)
• HOH (unmarried, qualifying person, maintaining home)

**Can't claim ignorance!**
If you prepare these returns, you must know the rules.

sections.title: Document Review
sections.content:
**Must review information provided:**

• Don't ignore red flags
• Information must be consistent
• Compare to prior years if available

**Red Flags:**
• Same address for unrelated individuals
• Questionable W-2s
• AGI inconsistent with claimed credits
• Same child claimed by multiple taxpayers
• Self-employment income with no expenses

sections.title: Reasonable Inquiry
sections.content:
**Ask questions when:**

• Information seems incorrect, inconsistent, or incomplete
• Red flags present
• Something doesn't add up

**Document the inquiry and response!**

**Cannot rely solely on client statements if:**
• Statements are inconsistent
• You have reason to doubt accuracy
• Industry knowledge suggests otherwise

sections.title: ⚠️ What Gets Preparers in Trouble
sections.content:
**Common Due Diligence Failures:**

• Not asking about absent parent
• Accepting verbal statements without probing
• Not verifying student enrollment for AOTC
• Ignoring W-2/1099 inconsistencies
• Not documenting reasonable cause for exceptions
• Same-day preparation without adequate inquiry

**IRS focuses on high-EIC ZIP codes!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: $635 penalty per credit/status for due diligence failure
Four items on one return = up to $2,540 penalty
Must complete Form 8867 and retain 3 years
Four requirements: Knowledge, Documents, Inquiry, Records
Cannot ignore red flags or inconsistencies
Document all inquiries and responses
Applies to EIC, CTC, AOTC, and HOH

knowledgeCheck.question: An enrolled agent prepares a tax return claiming the Head of Household filing status and the Child Tax Credit. The taxpayer provided all necessary documentation. Which of the following is TRUE regarding Form 8867?
options: Form 8867 is only required if the IRS requests it during an audit.
Form 8867 is required and must be completed, signed, and retained for three years from the return due date or filing date, whichever is later.
Form 8867 is only required if the taxpayer has a history of claiming ineligible credits.
Form 8867 is optional, but recommended, for returns claiming the Head of Household filing status or the Child Tax Credit.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Form 8867 is mandatory for returns claiming EIC, CTC/ACTC, AOTC, or HOH filing status. It must be completed, signed, and retained for three years from the return due date or filing date, whichever is later. This demonstrates due diligence.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the purpose of Form 8867 and the penalties for non-compliance.

reveal.question: What is the penalty for each failure to meet the due diligence requirements when preparing a return claiming EIC, CTC/ACTC, AOTC or HOH?
reveal.answer: The penalty is $635 per failure (for 2024). This applies for each instance of not meeting the due diligence requirements for each credit or filing status claimed.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios represents a potential 'red flag' that should prompt further investigation when preparing a return claiming the Earned Income Credit?
options: The taxpayer provides a W-2 from a well-known national company.
The taxpayer's AGI is consistent with the amount of earned income reported on their W-2.
The taxpayer uses the same address as several unrelated individuals, according to a publicly available database.
The taxpayer has claimed the EIC in prior years without issue.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sharing an address with several unrelated individuals is a common red flag for EIC and other credits, suggesting a potential scheme or misrepresentation of residency. This requires further investigation to ensure the taxpayer is eligible.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the types of situations that could indicate fraud or ineligibility.
